addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-engineering:design:design-implementation-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to verify that a UI implementation matches its Figma design specifications. This agent should be called after code has been written to implement a design, particularly after HTML/CSS/React components have been created or modified. The agent will visually compare the live implementation against the Figma design and provide detailed feedback on discrepancies.

Examples:
- <example>
  Context: The user has just implemented a new component based on a Figma design.
  user: "I've finished implementing the hero section based on the Figma design"
  assistant: "I'll review how well your implementation matches the Figma design."
  <commentary>
  Since UI implementation has been completed, use the design-implementation-reviewer agent to compare the live version with Figma.
  </commentary>
  </example>
- <example>
  Context: After the general code agent has implemented design changes.
  user: "Update the button styles to match the new design system"
  assistant: "I've updated the button styles. Now let me verify the implementation matches the Figma specifications."
  <commentary>
  After implementing design changes, proactively use the design-implementation-reviewer to ensure accuracy.
  </commentary>
  </example> (Tools: All tools)

- entire-engineering:design:design-iterator: Use this agent PROACTIVELY when design work isn't coming together on the first attempt. If you've made 1-2 design changes and the result still feels off, suggest using this agent with 5x or 10x iterations for deeper refinement. This agent takes screenshots, analyzes what's not working, implements improvements, and repeats N times to systematically fix design issues. Perfect when colors feel wrong, layouts aren't balanced, or the overall aesthetic needs work that single changes can't achieve. <example>Context: User asks to change button color but result still looks off. user: "Change the button to blue" assistant: [makes change, takes screenshot] "The button is now blue, but I notice the overall color balance still feels off. Would you like me to use the design-iterator agent with 5 iterations to systematically improve the color harmony and visual balance?" <commentary>PROACTIVE USE: After a simple change doesn't fully resolve the design issue, suggest the design-iterator to do deeper work.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User's design request leads to something that doesn't look right. user: "Make the hero section more modern" assistant: [makes initial changes] "I've added some modern elements, but the result isn't quite coming together. This looks like it needs iterative refinement. Let me use the design-iterator agent with 10 iterations to systematically improve typography, spacing, colors, and visual hierarchy until it feels polished." <commentary>PROACTIVE USE: When initial design work produces mediocre results, automatically suggest or use the design-iterator for comprehensive improvement.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User wants explicit iterative refinement. user: "The features section looks boring, iterate on it 10 times" assistant: "I'll use the design-iterator agent to systematically refine your features section through 10 iterations of visual improvements" <commentary>User explicitly requests iteration, use design-iterator directly.</commentary></example> <example>Context: Design task requires research and multiple passes. user: "Look at how Stripe does their pricing page and make mine better" assistant: "I'll launch the design-iterator agent with 8 iterations to research Stripe's design patterns and progressively apply those insights to your pricing page" <commentary>Competitor research combined with iterative refinement benefits from the systematic approach.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)

- entire-engineering:docs:ankane-readme-writer: Use this agent when you need to create or update README files following the Ankane-style template for Ruby gems. This includes writing concise documentation with imperative voice, keeping sentences under 15 words, organizing sections in the standard order (Installation, Quick Start, Usage, etc.), and ensuring proper formatting with single-purpose code fences and minimal prose. Examples: <example>Context: User is creating documentation for a new Ruby gem. user: "I need to write a README for my new search gem called 'turbo-search'" assistant: "I'll use the ankane-readme-writer agent to create a properly formatted README following the Ankane style guide" <commentary>Since the user needs a README for a Ruby gem and wants to follow best practices, use the ankane-readme-writer agent to ensure it follows the Ankane template structure.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User has an existing README that needs to be reformatted. user: "Can you update my gem's README to follow the Ankane style?" assistant: "Let me use the ankane-readme-writer agent to reformat your README according to the Ankane template" <commentary>The user explicitly wants to follow Ankane style, so use the specialized agent for this formatting standard.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)

- entire-engineering:review:data-integrity-guardian: Use this agent when you need to review database migrations, data models, or any code that manipulates persistent data. This includes checking migration safety, validating data constraints, ensuring transaction boundaries are correct, and verifying that referential integrity and privacy requirements are maintained. <example>Context: The user has just written a database migration that adds a new column and updates existing records. user: "I've created a migration to add a status column to the orders table" assistant: "I'll use the data-integrity-guardian agent to review this migration for safety and data integrity concerns" <commentary>Since the user has created a database migration, use the data-integrity-guardian agent to ensure the migration is safe, handles existing data properly, and maintains referential integrity.</commentary></example> <example>Context: The user has implemented a service that transfers data between models. user: "Here's my new service that moves user data from the legacy_users table to the new users table" assistant: "Let me have the data-integrity-guardian agent review this data transfer service" <commentary>Since this involves moving data between tables, the data-integrity-guardian should review transaction boundaries, data validation, and integrity preservation.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)

- entire-engineering:review:data-migration-expert: Use this agent when reviewing PRs that touch database migrations, data backfills, or any code that transforms production data. This agent validates ID mappings against production reality, checks for swapped values, verifies rollback safety, and ensures data integrity during schema changes. Essential for any migration that involves ID mappings, column renames, or data transformations. <example>Context: The user has a PR with database migrations that involve ID mappings. user: "Review this PR that migrates from action_id to action_module_name" assistant: "I'll use the data-migration-expert agent to validate the ID mappings and migration safety" <commentary>Since the PR involves ID mappings and data migration, use the data-migration-expert to verify the mappings match production and check for swapped values.</commentary></example> <example>Context: The user has a migration that transforms enum values. user: "This migration converts status integers to string enums" assistant: "Let me have the data-migration-expert verify the mapping logic and rollback safety" <commentary>Enum conversions are high-risk for swapped mappings, making this a perfect use case for data-migration-expert.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)

- entire-engineering:review:deployment-verification-agent: Use this agent when a PR touches production data, migrations, or any behavior that could silently discard or duplicate records. Produces a concrete pre/post-deploy checklist with SQL verification queries, rollback procedures, and monitoring plans. Essential for risky data changes where you need a Go/No-Go decision. <example>Context: The user has a PR that modifies how emails are classified. user: "This PR changes the classification logic, can you create a deployment checklist?" assistant: "I'll use the deployment-verification-agent to create a Go/No-Go checklist with verification queries" <commentary>Since the PR affects production data behavior, use deployment-verification-agent to create concrete verification and rollback plans.</commentary></example> <example>Context: The user is deploying a migration that backfills data. user: "We're about to deploy the user status backfill" assistant: "Let me create a deployment verification checklist with pre/post-deploy checks" <commentary>Backfills are high-risk deployments that need concrete verification plans and rollback procedures.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)

## plugin:entire-engineering:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. ## plugin:honeycomb:honeycomb
Honeycomb MCP Server - Observability Data Query Interface

<role>
You are an observability assistant for Honeycomb users. You investigate production behavior — performance, errors, deployment impact, alert state — by querying real telemetry through these tools. The workspace's actual state is only knowable through tool calls; do not reason from priors about what services, datasets, or alerts exist.

When the user names a dataset, span, or service, treat it as a starting point for discovery, not a license to skip it. The right span name within a dataset is rarely obvious from the dataset name alone, and aggregating the wrong attribute or the wrong span is the most common cause of misleading answers.
</role>

<workflow>
For most observability questions — including "how many", "what's slow", "what's erroring" — start with span discovery (list_spans, get_span_details). These tools return counts and attribute distributions directly; they often answer the question without a follow-up query. Reach for run_query only when you need a calculation, distribution, or comparison those tools can't express. Span tools work across all trace-aware datasets in scope without needing to know which dataset to target.

1. ORIENT
   - Call 'get_workspace_context' to learn the team, environments, and dataset counts. Use the returned environment slug in subsequent calls.

2. DISCOVER (pick the path that matches the question)
   - Span discovery — for "what's happening / what's slow / how many X / what does service X do":
     - 'list_spans' ranks span names by count with root_count to distinguish entry points from internal helpers. Counts here are often the answer to "how many X happened".
     - 'get_span_details' on a chosen span_name returns its populated attributes, value cardinality, and top observed values — including which spans actually populate a count-bearing attribute.
     Answer directly from these when you can — composing an equivalent run_query by hand is slower and more error-prone.

- honeycomb:metrics-queries: How to query OpenTelemetry metrics datasets in Honeycomb correctly. Metrics datasets follow different rules from trace/event datasets — many operations (bare COUNT, RATE_SUM, RATE_AVG, RATE_MAX, CONCURRENCY) are forbidden, temporal aggregation is automatic, and each metric has its own attributes. Use this skill when querying a metrics dataset (gauges, counters, histograms, sums), asking about temporal aggregation (RATE, INCREASE, SUMMARIZE, LAST), finding the metrics dataset or discovering metric names and attributes, debugging unexpected metrics query results, or querying infrastructure metrics like CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network stats. Do NOT use for instrumenting metrics (use otel-instrumentation), querying event datasets with "metrics" in their name, or conceptual questions (use observability-fundamentals).

- honeycomb:otel-migration: Guide for retrofitting OpenTelemetry into an existing, uninstrumented application. Trigger phrases: "migrate existing app to OTel", "add OpenTelemetry to existing project", "retrofit OTel into my codebase", "thread context through my code", "context propagation", "bridge Prometheus metrics to OTel", "logging bridge", "migrate logging to OTel", "slog bridge", "logback bridge", "verify my instrumentation", "traces are disconnected", "orphaned spans", "migrate to OpenTelemetry", "OTel migration plan", "how do I sequence an OTel migration", "add tracing to existing code", "refactor for context propagation", "Fiber context gotcha", "keep existing logging working with OTel", "add OTel without breaking Prometheus", "bridge existing metrics", "coexist with existing monitoring", or any request about retrofitting OpenTelemetry into an existing application. This skill is for migrating existing codebases, NOT greenfield instrumentation (use otel-instrumentation) or Beeline-specific migration (use beeline-migration).

This skill covers all dataset types except metrics datasets (dataset_type=metrics) — for those, use metrics-queries instead.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. - verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	package gitproto
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"context"
6		"crypto/tls"
7		"fmt"
8		"io"
9		"mime"
10		"net/http"
11		"net/http/httptrace"
12		"net/http/httputil"
13		"net/url"
14		"os"
15		"strings"
16	
17		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/useragent"
18		transporthttp "github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/transport/http"
19	)
20	
21	const maxHTTPErrorBody = 64 * 1024
22	
23	// diagnosticHeaders carry trace/correlation IDs that operators of upstream
24	// services use to look up the failing request server-side. Surfaced in
25	// httpError so a 500 with an opaque body (e.g. "Internal Server Error") still
26	// gives the user something actionable to share when reporting the failure.
27	var diagnosticHeaders = []string{
28		"Cf-Ray",
29		"X-Request-Id",
30		"Request-Id",
31		"X-Trace-Id",
32		"X-Amz-Request-Id",
33		"X-Github-Request-Id",
34		"Server",
35		"Content-Type",
36	}
37	
38	// httpError checks an HTTP response status and returns an error for non-2xx responses.
39	func httpError(res *http.Response) error {
40		if res.StatusCode >= http.StatusOK && res.StatusCode < http.StatusMultipleChoices {
41			return nil
42		}
43		var reason string
44		if res.Body != nil {
45			limited := io.LimitReader(res.Body, maxHTTPErrorBody+1)
46			data, err := io.ReadAll(limited)
47			if err == nil && len(data) > 0 {
48				if len(data) > maxHTTPErrorBody {
49					data = append(data[:maxHTTPErrorBody], []byte("...")...)
50				}
51				reason = strings.TrimSpace(string(data))
52			}
53		}
54		var diag []string
55		for _, h := range diagnosticHeaders {
56			if v := res.Header.Get(h); v != "" {
57				diag = append(diag, h+"="+v)
58			}
59		}
60		if len(diag) > 0 {
61			return fmt.Errorf("http %d: %s [%s] %s", res.StatusCode, res.Request.URL.Redacted(), strings.Join(diag, ", "), reason)
62		}
63		return fmt.Errorf("http %d: %s %s", res.StatusCode, res.Request.URL.Redacted(), reason)
64	}
65	
66	// StatsPhaseHeader is the HTTP header used to annotate requests with the
67	// current git-sync stats phase for round-trip tracking.
68	const StatsPhaseHeader = "X-Git-Sync-Stats-Phase"
69	
70	// HTTPTraceEnv enables verbose httptrace logging to stderr when set to any
71	// non-empty value other than "0" or "false". Diagnoses connection-pool
72	// behavior against hosts that close idle keep-alive connections more
73	// aggressively than Go's transport assumes (CDN edges, some hosted git
74	// providers) — a stale pooled connection surfaces as "use of closed network
75	// connection" on the next POST. Off by default; zero overhead unless set.
76	const HTTPTraceEnv = "GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE"
77	
78	func httpTraceEnabled() bool {
79		v := os.Getenv(HTTPTraceEnv)
80		if v == "" {
81			return false
82		}
83		switch strings.ToLower(v) {
84		case "0", "false", "no", "off":
85			return false
86		}
87		return true
88	}
89	
90	// withHTTPTrace returns ctx with a ClientTrace that logs connection lifecycle
91	// events for one request to stderr. label is prepended to every line so
92	// concurrent or interleaved requests stay readable. Returns ctx unchanged
93	// when GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE is not enabled.
94	func withHTTPTrace(ctx context.Context, label string) context.Context {
95		if !httpTraceEnabled() {
96			return ctx
97		}
98		trace := &httptrace.ClientTrace{
99			GetConn: func(hostPort string) {
100				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s GetConn %s\n", label, hostPort)
101			},
102			GotConn: func(info httptrace.GotConnInfo) {
103				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr,
104					"[httptrace] %s GotConn reused=%v wasIdle=%v idle=%s local=%s remote=%s\n",
105					label, info.Reused, info.WasIdle, info.IdleTime,
106					info.Conn.LocalAddr(), info.Conn.RemoteAddr())
107			},
108			PutIdleConn: func(err error) {
109				if err != nil {
110					fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s PutIdleConn err=%v\n", label, err)
111				} else {
112					fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s PutIdleConn ok\n", label)
113				}
114			},
115			ConnectStart: func(network, addr string) {
116				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s ConnectStart %s %s\n", label, network, addr)
117			},
118			ConnectDone: func(network, addr string, err error) {
119				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s ConnectDone %s %s err=%v\n", label, network, addr, err)
120			},
121			TLSHandshakeStart: func() {
122				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s TLSHandshakeStart\n", label)
123			},
124			TLSHandshakeDone: func(state tls.ConnectionState, err error) {
125				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s TLSHandshakeDone resumed=%v err=%v\n",
126					label, state.DidResume, err)
127			},
128			WroteRequest: func(info httptrace.WroteRequestInfo) {
129				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s WroteRequest err=%v\n", label, info.Err)
130			},
131		}
132		return httptrace.WithClientTrace(ctx, trace)
133	}
134	
135	// dumpOutgoingRequest prints the wire-format request line and headers for
136	// req to stderr, prefixed with label. The body is not consumed (passes
137	// body=false to httputil.DumpRequestOut), but Transfer-Encoding and
138	// Content-Length will reflect what Go's transport would actually send.
139	// Useful when a server behaves unexpectedly on a POST and you need to
140	// see what the request looked like at the protocol level — the
141	// connection-level trace tells you which TCP/TLS connection was used
142	// but not what was written on it. Best-effort: dump errors are
143	// surfaced as a single line so a transient dump failure doesn't mask
144	// the underlying request.
145	func dumpOutgoingRequest(req *http.Request, label string) {
146		dump, err := httputil.DumpRequestOut(req, false)
147		if err != nil {
148			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s dump error: %v\n", label, err)
149			return
150		}
151		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[httptrace] %s outgoing request:\n%s\n", label, redactAuthorization(dump))
152	}
153	
154	// redactAuthorization scrubs any Authorization header value from a dumped
155	// HTTP request so the credentials don't leak into stderr when
156	// GITSYNC_HTTP_TRACE is enabled in environments with shoulder-surfers,
157	// pasted-into-tickets logs, or shared shells.
158	func redactAuthorization(dump []byte) []byte {
159		const header = "Authorization:"
160		idx := bytes.Index(dump, []byte(header))
161		if idx < 0 {
162			return dump
163		}
164		end := bytes.IndexByte(dump[idx:], '\n')
165		if end < 0 {
166			end = len(dump) - idx
167		}
168		out := make([]byte, 0, len(dump))
169		out = append(out, dump[:idx]...)
170		out = append(out, []byte(header+" [REDACTED]")...)
171		out = append(out, dump[idx+end:]...)
172		return out
173	}
174	
175	// AuthMethod authorizes outbound HTTP requests for a remote. It is satisfied
176	// by *transporthttp.BasicAuth and *transporthttp.TokenAuth, whose Authorizer
177	// methods replaced the AuthMethod interface that go-git removed in v6 alpha.2.
178	type AuthMethod interface {
179		Authorizer(req *http.Request) error
180	}
181	
182	// CredentialHelper provides on-demand credentials when an HTTP request is
183	// rejected with 401. Lookup may block on user interaction if the underlying
184	// helper falls through to a terminal prompt — that's vanilla git's
185	// behaviour and intentional for interactive users. Callers that must not
186	// block (CI, daemons, the syncer's background loop) set
187	// GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0 in their environment, which the credential
188	// subprocess inherits. Lookup returns ok=false when no credentials could
189	// be obtained, so the caller can surface a clean 401.
190	//
191	// Approve/Reject are advisory and intentionally have no error return:
192	// failures must not poison the outer request flow.
193	type CredentialHelper interface {
194		Lookup(ctx context.Context, ep *url.URL) (username, password string, ok bool, err error)
195		Approve(ctx context.Context, ep *url.URL, username, password string)
196		Reject(ctx context.Context, ep *url.URL, username, password string)
197	}
198	
199	// HTTPConn represents a connection to a remote Git HTTP endpoint.
200	type HTTPConn struct {
201		Label       string
202		EndpointURL *url.URL
203		HTTP        *http.Client
204		Auth        AuthMethod
205	
206		// CredentialHelper, if set, is consulted on a 401 response when no
207		// initial Auth was configured. The retry happens once on /info/refs;
208		// on success the resolved credentials are stored in Auth for the
209		// remaining requests on this connection. Setting Auth up front
210		// disables the helper fallback — explicit auth wins.
211		CredentialHelper CredentialHelper
212	
213		// FollowInfoRefsRedirect, when true, rewrites Endpoint.Scheme and
214		// Endpoint.Host to the final URL returned by RequestInfoRefs after
215		// HTTP redirects. Subsequent PostRPC* calls then target the
216		// redirected host directly, matching vanilla git's smart-HTTP
217		// behaviour for discovery-aware servers that 307 /info/refs to a
218		// hosting replica. Endpoint.Path is never modified — it still
219		// contains the repo path. Off by default to preserve behaviour for
220		// callers that rely on Endpoint being stable.
221		FollowInfoRefsRedirect bool
222	
223		// InsecureSkipTLSVerify mirrors the same-named transport setting and
224		// must be set by callers whenever the HTTP client they pass in has
225		// TLS verification disabled. The credential-helper retry path uses it
226		// to refuse cross-host operations: with TLS verification off there's
227		// no way to know whether a redirect's destination is the host the
228		// user trusts or a MITM impersonating it, so sending the helper's
229		// stored credentials there is unsafe. Same-host 401s (no redirect)
230		// still retry — the user has accepted whatever host they configured.
231		InsecureSkipTLSVerify bool
232	
233		// ProgressOut is the destination for verbose sideband progress
234		// messages ("Enumerating objects: ...", "Resolving deltas: ..."
235		// streamed by upload-pack and receive-pack). Nil falls back to
236		// os.Stderr. Callers driving a live progress ticker can plug in a
237		// coordinated writer here so server-side progress lines don't
238		// clobber the in-place ticker frame.
239		ProgressOut io.Writer
240	
241		// pendingHelperCreds tracks credentials supplied by the helper via
242		// EnsureAuthForService but not yet validated against a real operation.
243		// The next RequestInfoRefs/PostRPCStreamBody approves on 2xx or rejects
244		// on 401/403, ensuring helper state reflects the actual outcome rather
245		// than an ambiguous probe response.
246		pendingHelperCreds *helperCreds
247	
248		// resolvedEndpoint, when non-nil, supersedes EndpointURL.Scheme/Host
249		// for outgoing requests on this conn. Populated when one of two
250		// redirect-following paths fires:
251		//
252		//   - The FollowInfoRefsRedirect block in RequestInfoRefs, after a
253		//     successful /info/refs that landed on a different host.
254		//   - adoptChallengeHost, after credential-helper auth resolves
255		//     against a cross-host challenge.
256		//
257		// Both are gated on FollowInfoRefsRedirect, so resolvedEndpoint only
258		// diverges from EndpointURL when the user has opted into following
259		// redirects. EndpointURL itself is never mutated — display, logging,
260		// telemetry, and the user-typed-URL accessors (Endpoint()) keep
261		// returning what the caller passed in.
262		//
263		// Path/userinfo are copied from EndpointURL; only Scheme/Host differ.
264		resolvedEndpoint *url.URL
265	}
266	
267	// requestURL returns the URL outgoing requests should build from on this
268	// conn. It is the resolved endpoint when one has been discovered (and the
269	// user opted into following redirects via FollowInfoRefsRedirect), and the
270	// user-typed EndpointURL otherwise.
271	func (c *HTTPConn) requestURL() *url.URL {
272		if c.resolvedEndpoint != nil {
273			return c.resolvedEndpoint
274		}
275		return c.EndpointURL
276	}
277	
278	type helperCreds struct {
279		user, pass string
280		url        *url.URL
281	}
282	
283	// NewHTTPConn creates a new connection to the given endpoint.
284	func NewHTTPConn(ep *url.URL, label string, auth AuthMethod, rt http.RoundTripper) *HTTPConn {
285		httpClient := &http.Client{Transport: rt}
286		return NewHTTPConnWithClient(ep, label, auth, httpClient)
287	}
288	
289	// NewHTTPConnWithClient creates a new connection using the provided HTTP client.
290	// Passing nil falls back to a default client and is intended only for direct
291	// callers outside git-sync's normal instrumented session setup.
292	func NewHTTPConnWithClient(ep *url.URL, label string, auth AuthMethod, httpClient *http.Client) *HTTPConn {
293		if httpClient == nil {
294			httpClient = &http.Client{Transport: http.DefaultTransport}
295		}
296		normalizeEndpointPath(ep)
297		return &HTTPConn{
298			Label:       label,
299			EndpointURL: ep,
300			HTTP:        httpClient,
301			Auth:        auth,
302		}
303	}
304	
305	func (c *HTTPConn) Endpoint() *url.URL { return c.EndpointURL }
306	
307	func (c *HTTPConn) ProgressWriter() io.Writer { return c.ProgressOut }
308	
309	func (c *HTTPConn) SetProgressWriter(w io.Writer) { c.ProgressOut = w }
310	
311	func (c *HTTPConn) Close() error { return nil }
312	
313	func normalizeEndpointPath(ep *url.URL) {
314		if ep == nil {
315			return
316		}
317		ep.Path = strings.TrimRight(ep.Path, "/")
318		ep.RawPath = strings.TrimRight(ep.RawPath, "/")
319	}
320	
321	// NewHTTPTransport returns the default git-sync HTTP transport. It clones
322	// http.DefaultTransport so config changes (TLS, keep-alive policy) don't
323	// leak into other code in the same process.
324	//
325	// Keep-alives are disabled. The git smart-HTTP workflow over the same host
326	// is coarse-grained — info/refs, then a single upload-pack or receive-pack
327	// POST — with real work in between (planning, source fetch, local object
328	// materialization). On the push side that gap is long enough for CDN
329	// edges and some hosted git providers to close their end of an idle TLS
330	// socket; the next POST then fails with "use of closed network connection"
331	// because the pooled connection is half-dead. Pool reuse would save at
332	// most one TLS handshake per sync, which is negligible against multi-MB
333	// to multi-GB transfers, so we prefer a fresh connection per request and
334	// avoid the race entirely.
335	//
336	// Library callers that need pool reuse (e.g. embedding git-sync in a
337	// long-running process that hits the same host repeatedly with short
338	// gaps) can pass their own RoundTripper to NewHTTPConn instead.
339	func NewHTTPTransport(skipTLS bool) http.RoundTripper {
340		base, ok := http.DefaultTransport.(*http.Transport)
341		if !ok {
342			return http.DefaultTransport
343		}
344		tc := base.Clone()
345		tc.DisableKeepAlives = true
346		if skipTLS {
347			if tc.TLSClientConfig == nil {
348				tc.TLSClientConfig = &tls.Config{MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS12}
349			}
350			tc.TLSClientConfig.InsecureSkipVerify = true
351		}
352		return tc
353	}
354	
355	// RequestInfoRefs fetches /info/refs for the given service.
356	func RequestInfoRefs(ctx context.Context, conn Conn, service string, gitProtocol string) ([]byte, error) {
357		data, err := conn.RequestInfoRefs(ctx, service, gitProtocol)
358		if err != nil {
359			return nil, fmt.Errorf("request info refs: %w", err)
360		}
361		return data, nil
362	}
363	
364	// RequestInfoRefs fetches /info/refs for the given service.
365	func (c *HTTPConn) RequestInfoRefs(ctx context.Context, service string, gitProtocol string) ([]byte, error) {
366		res, err := c.doInfoRefsRequest(ctx, service, gitProtocol, c.Auth, nil)
367		if err != nil {
368			return nil, err
369		}
370		res, err = c.tryHelperRetry(ctx, res, func(auth AuthMethod, target *url.URL) (*http.Response, error) {
371			return c.doInfoRefsRequest(ctx, service, gitProtocol, auth, target)
372		})
373		if err != nil {
374			return nil, err
375		}
376		defer res.Body.Close()
377	
378		data, err := c.readInfoRefsResponse(res, service)
379		// Settle helper credentials on the fully-validated outcome: approve only
380		// once the advertisement parsed and read within limits, reject on 401/403.
381		// Running this after validation stops a misleading 2xx (wrong content-type
382		// or oversized body) from persisting credentials for an operation that
383		// ultimately failed.
384		c.resolvePendingHelperCreds(ctx, res, err == nil)
385		if err != nil {
386			return nil, err
387		}
388		return data, nil
389	}

391	// readInfoRefsResponse validates and reads an /info/refs response: it checks
392	// the HTTP status and advertisement content-type, applies any redirect to the
393	// endpoint, and reads the body under a size cap. The caller closes res.Body.
394	func (c *HTTPConn) readInfoRefsResponse(res *http.Response, service string) ([]byte, error) {
395		if err := httpError(res); err != nil {
396			return nil, err
397		}
398		wantContentType := fmt.Sprintf("application/x-%s-advertisement", service)
399		gotContentType := res.Header.Get("Content-Type")
400		gotMediaType := gotContentType
401		if gotContentType != "" {
402			if mediaType, _, err := mime.ParseMediaType(gotContentType); err == nil {
403				gotMediaType = mediaType
404			}
405		}
406		if gotMediaType != wantContentType {
407			return nil, fmt.Errorf("unexpected info/refs content-type %q, want %q", gotContentType, wantContentType)
408		}
409		if c.FollowInfoRefsRedirect && res.Request != nil && res.Request.URL != nil {
410			final := res.Request.URL
411			current := c.requestURL()
412			if final.Host != current.Host || final.Scheme != current.Scheme {
413				// Don't mutate EndpointURL — record the resolved endpoint in
414				// a separate field so the user-typed URL stays available for
415				// display/logging/telemetry. Path/userinfo carry over from
416				// EndpointURL (same assumption as before: the redirect target
417				// serves the same repo path).
418				resolved := *c.EndpointURL
419				resolved.Scheme = final.Scheme
420				resolved.Host = final.Host
421				c.resolvedEndpoint = &resolved
422			}
423		}
424		// Bound the read to prevent unbounded memory allocation (issue #9).
425		const maxInfoRefsSize = 64 * 1024 * 1024 // 64 MiB
426		lr := io.LimitReader(res.Body, maxInfoRefsSize+1)
427		data, err := io.ReadAll(lr)
428		if err != nil {
429			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read info-refs response: %w", err)
430		}
431		if int64(len(data)) > maxInfoRefsSize {
432			return nil, fmt.Errorf("info/refs response exceeds %d byte limit", maxInfoRefsSize)
433		}
434		return data, nil
435	}

437	// PostRPC sends a buffered POST to the given service and returns the full response body.
438	// Responses are bounded to prevent unbounded memory allocation (issue #9).
439	func PostRPC(ctx context.Context, conn Conn, service string, body []byte, v2 bool, phase string) ([]byte, error) {
440		reader, err := PostRPCStream(ctx, conn, service, body, v2, phase)
441		if err != nil {
442			return nil, err
443		}
444		defer reader.Close()
445		const maxRPCResponse = 128 * 1024 * 1024 // 128 MiB
446		lr := io.LimitReader(reader, maxRPCResponse+1)
447		data, err := io.ReadAll(lr)
448		if err != nil {
449			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read RPC response: %w", err)
450		}
451		if int64(len(data)) > maxRPCResponse {
452			return nil, fmt.Errorf("RPC response for %s exceeds %d byte limit", service, maxRPCResponse)
453		}
454		return data, nil
455	}

457	// PostRPCStream sends a POST to the given service and returns the response body
458	// as a streaming reader. Caller must close the returned ReadCloser.
459	func PostRPCStream(ctx context.Context, conn Conn, service string, body []byte, v2 bool, phase string) (io.ReadCloser, error) {
460		return PostRPCStreamBody(ctx, conn, service, bytes.NewReader(body), v2, phase)
461	}
462	
463	// PostRPCStreamBody sends a POST to the given service using a streaming request body.
464	// Caller must close the returned ReadCloser.
465	func PostRPCStreamBody(ctx context.Context, conn Conn, service string, body io.Reader, v2 bool, phase string) (io.ReadCloser, error) {
466		reader, err := conn.PostRPCStreamBody(ctx, service, body, v2, phase)
467		if err != nil {
468			return nil, fmt.Errorf("post RPC stream body: %w", err)
469		}
470		return reader, nil
471	}

473	// PostRPCStreamBody sends a POST to the given service using a streaming request body.
474	// Caller must close the returned ReadCloser.
475	//
476	// The body is sent as-is — streaming readers produce a chunked request.
477	//
478	// On a 401 we consult the credential helper and retry, mirroring git's
479	// own behaviour for servers that allow anonymous /info/refs but gate the
480	// actual upload-pack/receive-pack POST behind auth. Retry is only possible
481	// when body is an io.Seeker (so we can rewind it); callers that pass a raw
482	// non-seekable Reader will see the 401 surface as-is.
483	func (c *HTTPConn) PostRPCStreamBody(ctx context.Context, service string, body io.Reader, v2 bool, phase string) (io.ReadCloser, error) {
484		res, err := c.doPostRPCRequest(ctx, service, body, v2, phase, c.Auth, nil)
485		if err != nil {
486			return nil, err
487		}
488		if seeker, ok := body.(io.Seeker); ok {
489			res, err = c.tryHelperRetry(ctx, res, func(auth AuthMethod, target *url.URL) (*http.Response, error) {
490				if _, seekErr := seeker.Seek(0, io.SeekStart); seekErr != nil {
491					return nil, fmt.Errorf("rewind RPC body for credential-helper retry: %w", seekErr)
492				}
493				return c.doPostRPCRequest(ctx, service, body, v2, phase, auth, target)
494			})
495			if err != nil {
496				return nil, err
497			}
498		}
499		httpErr := httpError(res)
500		// Settle helper credentials on the validated status: approve on a 2xx,
501		// reject on 401/403. For the POST path the HTTP status is the whole
502		// success signal — there's no advertisement body to validate further.
503		c.resolvePendingHelperCreds(ctx, res, httpErr == nil)
504		if httpErr != nil {
505			_ = res.Body.Close()
506			return nil, httpErr
507		}
508		return res.Body, nil
509	}
510	
511	// doPostRPCRequest issues a single POST to /<service>. Caller closes res.Body.
512	//
513	// target is an optional override URL: when non-nil, the request is sent
514	// verbatim to that URL instead of building one from c.EndpointURL. See
515	// doInfoRefsRequest for why — same redirect-strip avoidance.
516	func (c *HTTPConn) doPostRPCRequest(ctx context.Context, service string, body io.Reader, v2 bool, phase string, auth AuthMethod, target *url.URL) (*http.Response, error) {
517		var reqURL string
518		if target != nil {
519			reqURL = target.String()
520		} else {
521			reqURL = fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", c.requestURL().String(), service)
522		}
523		ctx = withHTTPTrace(ctx, "POST "+service)
524	
525		req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodPost, reqURL, body)
526		if err != nil {
527			return nil, fmt.Errorf("create RPC request: %w", err)
528		}
529		req.Header.Set("Content-Type", fmt.Sprintf("application/x-%s-request", service))
530		req.Header.Set("Accept", fmt.Sprintf("application/x-%s-result", service))
531		req.Header.Set("User-Agent", useragent.GoGit())
532		req.Header.Set(StatsPhaseHeader, phase)
533		if v2 {
534			req.Header.Set("Git-Protocol", GitProtocolV2)
535		}
536		ApplyAuth(req, auth)
537	
538		if httpTraceEnabled() {
539			dumpOutgoingRequest(req, "POST "+service)
540		}
541	
542		res, err := c.HTTP.Do(req)
543		if err != nil {
544			return nil, fmt.Errorf("post RPC: %w", err)
545		}
546		return res, nil
547	}
548	
549	// ApplyAuth applies the given auth method to an HTTP request. Errors from
550	// the Authorizer (e.g. transient signing failures) are surfaced as request
551	// failures by leaving the Authorization header unset; the upstream server
552	// will reject with 401 and the caller logs the surrounding context.
553	func ApplyAuth(req *http.Request, auth AuthMethod) {
554		if auth == nil {
555			return
556		}
557		_ = auth.Authorizer(req) //nolint:errcheck // BasicAuth and TokenAuth never error; future authorizers should surface 401s instead
558	}
559	
560	// EnsureAuthForService tentatively attaches helper credentials before a
561	// non-rewindable request body is committed. It's a no-op when no helper
562	// is configured, when Auth is already set, or when the helper has no
563	// credentials to offer.
564	//
565	// Used from push.go (and other streaming-body POST paths) where the body
566	// is built from a live upstream stream (e.g. io.MultiReader over a pack
567	// reader) and can't be replayed on a mid-stream 401.
568	//
569	// The flow is:
570	//  1. Probe with a POST to /<service> using the smart-HTTP flush packet
571	//     "0000" as body — a valid no-op (zero ref updates, zero pack data)
572	//     by spec. We probe with POST rather than GET because the auth layer
573	//     may only gate the POST handler; a GET probe would slip past on
574	//     servers that 404/405 GET while requiring auth on POST.
575	//  2. If the probe gets 401, ask the helper for credentials keyed on the
576	//     actually-challenged host (which may differ from c.EndpointURL after a
577	//     cross-host redirect). Keying on the post-redirect host matters: that's
578	//     where the user stored their creds, and that's the key we'll later
579	//     Approve/Reject against.
580	//  3. Attach the credentials tentatively. The next real operation calls
581	//     resolvePendingHelperCreds, which Approves them only once that
582	//     operation fully succeeds or Rejects them on 401/403 — helper state
583	//     only changes based on the actual outcome, never on the probe response
584	//     alone.
585	//  4. If the challenge came from a cross-host redirect, rewrite
586	//     c.EndpointURL's scheme/host to the challenger so the real op skips the
587	//     redirect (which Go's http.Client would otherwise follow with the
588	//     Authorization header stripped, turning every push into a fresh 401).
589	//
590	// If the probe doesn't 401 (200, 404, 405, etc.) we don't attach; the
591	// server either accepts anonymous POSTs here or returns ambiguously,
592	// and either way attaching unvalidated credentials could leak them.
593	func (c *HTTPConn) EnsureAuthForService(ctx context.Context, service string) {
594		if c.Auth != nil || c.CredentialHelper == nil {
595			return
596		}
597		res, err := c.doServiceProbe(ctx, service)
598		if err != nil {
599			return
600		}
601		defer res.Body.Close()
602		if res.StatusCode != http.StatusUnauthorized {
603			return
604		}
605		challengeURL := challengeURLFor(c.EndpointURL, res)
606		if c.InsecureSkipTLSVerify && challengeURL.Host != c.requestURL().Host {
607			// See tryHelperRetry: with TLS verification off we can't tell a
608			// real challenger from a MITM, so we won't attach helper creds
609			// after a cross-host probe redirect. The real op will surface
610			// the 401 and the user can resolve it.
611			return
612		}
613		user, pass, ok, lookupErr := c.CredentialHelper.Lookup(ctx, challengeURL)
614		if lookupErr != nil || !ok {
615			return
616		}
617		c.Auth = &transporthttp.BasicAuth{Username: user, Password: pass}
618		c.pendingHelperCreds = &helperCreds{user: user, pass: pass, url: challengeURL}
619		c.adoptChallengeHost(challengeURL)
620	}
621	
622	// resolvePendingHelperCreds settles credentials that were attached tentatively
623	// — either by EnsureAuthForService's probe or by a tryHelperRetry that got a
624	// 2xx — based on the fully-validated outcome of a real operation. Called from
625	// RequestInfoRefs and PostRPCStreamBody. No-op if nothing is pending.
626	//
627	// success reports whether the operation actually succeeded (a 2xx whose body
628	// also passed any service-specific validation), as opposed to merely returning
629	// a 2xx status. We approve only on success, so a misleading 2xx — e.g. an
630	// /info/refs response with the wrong content-type or an oversized body — can't
631	// persist credentials for an operation the caller still reports as failed.
632	func (c *HTTPConn) resolvePendingHelperCreds(ctx context.Context, res *http.Response, success bool) {
633		if c.pendingHelperCreds == nil || c.CredentialHelper == nil {
634			return
635		}
636		creds := c.pendingHelperCreds
637		switch {
638		case success:
639			c.pendingHelperCreds = nil
640			c.CredentialHelper.Approve(ctx, creds.url, creds.user, creds.pass)
641		case res.StatusCode == http.StatusUnauthorized || res.StatusCode == http.StatusForbidden:
642			c.pendingHelperCreds = nil
643			c.Auth = nil
644			c.CredentialHelper.Reject(ctx, creds.url, creds.user, creds.pass)
645		}
646		// Otherwise (e.g. a 2xx with a malformed/oversized body): leave the creds
647		// pending and c.Auth in place. We must not approve credentials for an
648		// operation that failed validation, but a non-auth failure isn't proof
649		// they're bad either. The conn is short-lived (one sync), so leftover
650		// pending state at end of life is harmless.
651	}
652	
653	// flushPacket is the smart-HTTP pkt-line "flush" marker. A request body
654	// containing only a flush packet is a valid no-op for both upload-pack
655	// (no wants/haves) and receive-pack (no ref updates, no pack data).
656	var flushPacket = []byte("0000")
657	
658	func (c *HTTPConn) doServiceProbe(ctx context.Context, service string) (*http.Response, error) {
659		reqURL := fmt.Sprintf("%s/%s", c.requestURL().String(), service)
660		req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodPost, reqURL, bytes.NewReader(flushPacket))
661		if err != nil {
662			return nil, fmt.Errorf("create auth-probe request: %w", err)
663		}
664		req.Header.Set("Content-Type", fmt.Sprintf("application/x-%s-request", service))
665		req.Header.Set("Accept", fmt.Sprintf("application/x-%s-result", service))
666		req.Header.Set("User-Agent", useragent.GoGit())
667		req.Header.Set(StatsPhaseHeader, service+" auth-probe")
668		res, err := c.HTTP.Do(req)
669		if err != nil {
670			return nil, fmt.Errorf("auth-probe request: %w", err)
671		}
672		return res, nil
673	}
674	
675	// tryHelperRetry handles the 401 → lookup → retry → approve/reject lifecycle
676	// when a CredentialHelper is configured and no explicit Auth was set up front
677	// (explicit auth must surface its own failures rather than be quietly papered
678	// over). retry attempts the same request with helper-supplied credentials,
679	// targeted at the URL that actually returned the 401 (which may differ from
680	// c.EndpointURL if Go's http.Client followed a cross-host redirect).
681	//
682	// On a 2xx retry the credentials are stored on c.Auth (so follow-up calls on
683	// the same connection reuse them) and recorded as pending — the caller then
684	// approves them via resolvePendingHelperCreds once the response passes full
685	// validation, never on the 2xx status alone. If the challenge was on a host
686	// different from c.EndpointURL we also rewrite c.EndpointURL's scheme/host to
687	// the challenger so subsequent ops on this conn don't redirect again (Go's
688	// http.Client strips Authorization on cross-host redirects, which would
689	// otherwise turn every follow-up into a fresh 401 → Reject of valid creds).
690	//
691	// On retry failure (401, 403, or transport error) the helper is told to
692	// reject the credentials immediately so a stale stored token self-heals on
693	// the next run.
694	//
695	// Caller is responsible for closing the returned response body.
696	func (c *HTTPConn) tryHelperRetry(ctx context.Context, res *http.Response, retry func(AuthMethod, *url.URL) (*http.Response, error)) (*http.Response, error) {
697		if res.StatusCode != http.StatusUnauthorized || c.Auth != nil || c.CredentialHelper == nil {
698			return res, nil
699		}
700		challengeURL := challengeURLFor(c.EndpointURL, res)
701		if c.InsecureSkipTLSVerify && challengeURL.Host != c.requestURL().Host {
702			// Refuse to hand helper-stored credentials to a redirect target we
703			// can't authenticate. With TLS verification off, the post-redirect
704			// host could be anyone presenting a self-signed cert for the host
705			// our Lookup key would hand creds to. Let the 401 surface so the
706			// user fixes their setup (don't combine SkipTLSVerify with a
707			// credential helper on a redirecting endpoint).
708			return res, nil
709		}
710		user, pass, ok, lookupErr := c.CredentialHelper.Lookup(ctx, challengeURL)
711		if lookupErr != nil {
712			_ = res.Body.Close()
713			return nil, fmt.Errorf("look up credentials: %w", lookupErr)
714		}
715		if !ok {
716			return res, nil
717		}
718		// Capture the actually-challenged URL — what http.Client landed on after
719		// any redirects, including its path/query — so the retry hits it directly
720		// instead of replaying through c.EndpointURL and getting Authorization
721		// stripped on the cross-host hop.
722		retryTarget := res.Request.URL
723		_ = res.Body.Close()
724		retryAuth := &transporthttp.BasicAuth{Username: user, Password: pass}
725		res, err := retry(retryAuth, retryTarget)
726		if err != nil {
727			c.CredentialHelper.Reject(ctx, challengeURL, user, pass)
728			return nil, err
729		}
730		switch {
731		case res.StatusCode == http.StatusUnauthorized || res.StatusCode == http.StatusForbidden:
732			// 403 included because some token services (e.g. Cloudflare)
733			// surface "Invalid or expired token" as 403 rather than 401.
734			c.CredentialHelper.Reject(ctx, challengeURL, user, pass)
735		case res.StatusCode >= http.StatusOK && res.StatusCode < http.StatusMultipleChoices:
736			// Attach tentatively and defer approval to resolvePendingHelperCreds,
737			// which runs after the caller validates the response body — a 2xx
738			// status alone isn't proof the operation succeeded.
739			c.Auth = retryAuth
740			c.pendingHelperCreds = &helperCreds{user: user, pass: pass, url: challengeURL}
741			c.adoptChallengeHost(challengeURL)
742		}
743		return res, nil
744	}
745	
746	// adoptChallengeHost records the host that just successfully authenticated
747	// as this conn's resolved endpoint, so subsequent ops on the same conn go
748	// there directly instead of replaying through c.EndpointURL — which would
749	// redirect again and have its Authorization header stripped on the cross-
750	// host hop, turning every follow-up into a fresh 401.
751	//
752	// Gated on FollowInfoRefsRedirect: the user explicitly opting into redirect-
753	// following is the trigger for the conn's effective endpoint changing. With
754	// the flag off the immediate retry still hits the challenger directly (so
755	// the current op succeeds and creds get Approved on the right key), but the
756	// next op stays pointed at the user-typed URL. EndpointURL itself is never
757	// mutated; we set resolvedEndpoint instead, leaving EndpointURL as user
758	// input for display/logging/telemetry to read.
759	//
760	// Path/userinfo are copied from EndpointURL — same assumption
761	// FollowInfoRefsRedirect's /info/refs block makes about the redirect target
762	// serving the same repo path.
763	func (c *HTTPConn) adoptChallengeHost(challengeURL *url.URL) {
764		if !c.FollowInfoRefsRedirect || challengeURL == nil {
765			return
766		}
767		current := c.requestURL()
768		if challengeURL.Host == current.Host && challengeURL.Scheme == current.Scheme {
769			return
770		}
771		resolved := *c.EndpointURL
772		resolved.Scheme = challengeURL.Scheme
773		resolved.Host = challengeURL.Host
774		c.resolvedEndpoint = &resolved
775	}
776	
777	// challengeURLFor returns the URL key used to query the credential helper
778	// for an auth challenge. After a 3xx the actually-challenged host is in
779	// res.Request.URL, which may differ from c.EndpointURL — using the wrong
780	// one would query (and possibly approve/reject) credentials under the
781	// wrong helper key. The original repo path is preserved so the key still
782	// matches what the user configured.
783	//
784	// We deliberately key on orig.Path rather than res.Request.URL.Path so
785	// credentials the user stored against the URL they typed remain findable
786	// when a redirect rewrites paths (e.g. github.com/owner/repo redirected
787	// to cdn.example/mirror/owner/repo). Trade-off: for path-aware helpers
788	// (credential.useHttpPath=true) the Approve/Reject key may be less
789	// precise than the actual challenge URL. Not a credential leak — creds
790	// only ever reach hosts the user already trusted enough to store them
791	// against — just a helper-audit-trail imprecision.
792	func challengeURLFor(orig *url.URL, res *http.Response) *url.URL {
793		if res == nil || res.Request == nil || res.Request.URL == nil {
794			return orig
795		}
796		final := res.Request.URL
797		if final.Host == orig.Host && final.Scheme == orig.Scheme {
798			return orig
799		}
800		out := *orig
801		out.Scheme = final.Scheme
802		out.Host = final.Host
803		return &out
804	}
805	
806	// doInfoRefsRequest issues a single /info/refs GET. Caller closes res.Body.
807	//
808	// target is an optional override URL: when non-nil, the request is sent
809	// verbatim to that URL instead of building one from c.EndpointURL. Used by
810	// the credential-helper retry path to hit a redirected challenge host
811	// directly, skipping the redirect that would otherwise cause Go's
812	// http.Client to strip the Authorization header on the cross-host hop.
813	func (c *HTTPConn) doInfoRefsRequest(ctx context.Context, service, gitProtocol string, auth AuthMethod, target *url.URL) (*http.Response, error) {
814		var reqURL string
815		if target != nil {
816			reqURL = target.String()
817		} else {
818			reqURL = fmt.Sprintf("%s/info/refs?service=%s", c.requestURL().String(), service)
819		}
820		ctx = withHTTPTrace(ctx, "GET "+service+"/info/refs")
821		req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodGet, reqURL, nil)
822		if err != nil {
823			return nil, fmt.Errorf("create info-refs request: %w", err)
824		}
825		req.Header.Set("Accept", "*/*")
826		req.Header.Set("User-Agent", useragent.GoGit())
827		req.Header.Set(StatsPhaseHeader, service+" info-refs")
828		if gitProtocol != "" {
829			req.Header.Set("Git-Protocol", gitProtocol)
830		}
831		ApplyAuth(req, auth)
832		res, err := c.HTTP.Do(req)
833		if err != nil {
834			return nil, fmt.Errorf("request info-refs: %w", err)
835		}
836		return res, nil
837	}
