1	// Package bootstrap implements the bootstrap relay strategy for git-sync.
2	// This handles initial seeding of an empty target, both one-shot and batched.
3	package bootstrap
4	
5	import (
6		"bytes"
7		"context"
8		"encoding/json"
9		"errors"
10		"fmt"
11		"io"
12		"log/slog"
13		"net/http"
14		"regexp"
15		"strconv"
16		"strings"
17		"sync"
18	
19		git "github.com/go-git/go-git/v6"
20		"github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing"
21	
22		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/convert"
23		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/gitproto"
24		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/planner"
25		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/useragent"
26	)
27	
28	const (
29		defaultTargetMaxPackBytes  = 512 * 1024 * 1024
30		githubLargeRepoThresholdKB = 1536 * 1024
31	)
32	
33	var bodyLimitPattern = regexp.MustCompile(`body exceeded size limit ([0-9]+)`)
34	
35	// GitHubRepoAPIBaseURL is the base for GitHub API calls (replaceable in tests).
36	var GitHubRepoAPIBaseURL = "https://api.github.com"
37	
38	// Params holds the inputs for a bootstrap execution.
39	type Params struct {
40		SourceConn    gitproto.Conn
41		SourceService interface {
42			FetchPack(ctx context.Context, conn gitproto.Conn, desired map[plumbing.ReferenceName]gitproto.DesiredRef, haves map[plumbing.ReferenceName]plumbing.Hash) (io.ReadCloser, error)
43			FetchCommitParents(ctx context.Context, conn gitproto.Conn, ref gitproto.DesiredRef, haves []plumbing.Hash) (map[plumbing.Hash][]plumbing.Hash, error)
44			SupportsBootstrapBatch() bool
45		}
46		TargetPusher interface {
47			PushPack(ctx context.Context, cmds []gitproto.PushCommand, pack io.ReadCloser) error
48			PushCommands(ctx context.Context, cmds []gitproto.PushCommand) error
49		}
50		DesiredRefs map[plumbing.ReferenceName]planner.DesiredRef
51		TargetRefs  map[plumbing.ReferenceName]plumbing.Hash
52		// SourceHeadTarget is the source ref that HEAD points to, when advertised.
53		// Empty if unknown. When set, batched bootstrap plans this branch first and
54		// uses its commit-graph reachability as a cutoff for subsequent branches.
55		SourceHeadTarget plumbing.ReferenceName
56		MaxPackBytes     int64
57		TargetMaxPack    int64
58		Verbose          bool
59		Logger           *slog.Logger
60		// Strategy selects the chain ordering for batched bootstrap. Empty
61		// or "first-parent" walks the first-parent backbone (default,
62		// matches historical behaviour). "topo" includes every reachable
63		// commit in topological order so sub-pack boundaries can land
64		// inside merge-pulled side branches — useful for repos like
65		// "checkpoint" branches where each first-parent step drags in a
66		// large second-parent ancestry that would otherwise have to be
67		// pushed in one indivisible pack.
68		//
69		// Server requirement under "topo": successive checkpoints aren't
70		// always in an ancestor-descendant relationship (topological
71		// order can interleave parallel branches), so the internal
72		// refs/gitsync/bootstrap/heads/<branch> temp ref may receive
73		// non-fast-forward updates between checkpoints. The temp ref is
74		// purely internal scaffolding — user-visible refs (refs/heads,
75		// refs/tags) only receive a single fast-forward update at
76		// cutover — but targets that enforce receive.denyNonFastforwards
77		// across all refs (not just refs/heads) will reject these
78		// updates and fail the bootstrap. Major hosts (GitHub, GitLab,
79		// Bitbucket, Cloudflare) do not enable this by default; the
80		// constraint only matters on hardened/locked-down deployments.
81		Strategy string
82		// OnPhase, when non-nil, is called with a short human-readable label
83		// describing the current bootstrap activity (e.g. "pack 3/8") so a
84		// live progress renderer can surface what is currently in flight.
85		// Called from the goroutine driving Execute; implementations must not
86		// block.
87		OnPhase func(string)
88		// OnNotice, when non-nil, receives one-time human-readable messages
89		// about discrete events worth surfacing alongside progress (pack
90		// subdivision, switching to batched mode). Implementations should
91		// treat each call as one log line.
92		OnNotice func(string)
93	}
94	
95	func (p Params) notice(msg string) {
96		if p.OnNotice != nil {
97			p.OnNotice(msg)
98		}
99	}
100	
101	// Result holds the outcome of the bootstrap strategy. Pushed is the count
102	// of attempted ref creates; under BestEffort, callers that want a count
103	// excluding rejected refs need to consult Pusher.OnRejection or apply the
104	// same downgrade pass the syncer wrapper does.
105	type Result struct {
106		Plans             []planner.BranchPlan
107		Pushed            int
108		Relay             bool
109		RelayMode         string
110		RelayReason       string
111		Batching          bool
112		BatchCount        int
113		PlannedBatchCount int
114		TempRefs          []string
115	}
116	
117	type plannedBatch struct {
118		planner.BootstrapBatch
119	
120		chain []plumbing.Hash // full first-parent chain (root→tip) for subdividing on push failure
121		// subsumed is true when the branch tip is already reachable from the
122		// trunk (planned first), so every object is already on the target after
123		// trunk's batches. Execution skips the commit-graph fetch, the pack
124		// fetch, the temp ref, and the pack push — emitting only a single ref
125		// create command.
126		subsumed bool
127	}
128	
129	// Execute runs the bootstrap strategy (one-shot or batched).
130	func Execute(ctx context.Context, p Params, relayReason string) (Result, error) {
131		if p.TargetPusher == nil {
132			return Result{Relay: true, RelayMode: "bootstrap", RelayReason: relayReason}, errors.New("bootstrap strategy requires TargetPusher")
133		}
134	
135		// GitHub large-repo preflight
136		if batchLimit, ok := githubBatchLimit(ctx, p); ok {
137			p.TargetMaxPack = batchLimit
138			p.log("bootstrap github preflight selected batched mode",
139				"target_max_pack_bytes", p.TargetMaxPack)
140		}
141	
142		planTargetRefs := p.TargetRefs
143		if p.TargetMaxPack > 0 {
144			planTargetRefs = adjustedBootstrapTargetRefs(p.DesiredRefs, p.TargetRefs)
145		}
146		plans, err := planner.BuildBootstrapPlans(p.DesiredRefs, planTargetRefs)
147		if err != nil {
148			return Result{}, fmt.Errorf("build bootstrap plans: %w", err)
149		}
150	
151		result := Result{
152			Plans: plans, Relay: true, RelayMode: "bootstrap", RelayReason: relayReason,
153		}
154	
155		if p.TargetMaxPack > 0 {
156			return executeBatched(ctx, p, plans, result)
157		}
158	
159		// One-shot bootstrap
160		p.log("bootstrap fetching refs from source", "ref_count", len(plans))
161		gpDesired := convert.DesiredRefs(p.DesiredRefs)
162		packReader, err := p.SourceService.FetchPack(ctx, p.SourceConn, gpDesired, nil)
163		if err != nil {
164			if errors.Is(err, git.NoErrAlreadyUpToDate) {
165				return result, nil
166			}
167			return result, fmt.Errorf("fetch source pack: %w", err)
168		}
169		packReader = gitproto.LimitPackReader(packReader, p.MaxPackBytes)
170		packReader = closeOnce(packReader)
171	
172		p.log("bootstrap pushing refs to target", "ref_count", len(plans))
173		if p.OnPhase != nil {
174			p.OnPhase("pushing pack")
175		}
176		cmds := convert.PlansToPushCommands(hoistSourceHeadPlan(plans, p.SourceHeadTarget), false)
177		pushErr := p.TargetPusher.PushPack(ctx, cmds, packReader)
178		_ = packReader.Close()
179		if pushErr != nil {
180			autoBatch, ok := autoTargetMaxPackBytes(p, pushErr)
181			if !ok {
182				return result, fmt.Errorf("push target refs: %w", pushErr)
183			}
184			p.log("bootstrap retrying with batched mode after target rejection",
185				"target_max_pack_bytes", autoBatch)
186			p.notice(fmt.Sprintf("target rejected pack — switching to batched mode (limit %s)",
187				humanBytes(autoBatch)))
188			p.TargetMaxPack = autoBatch
189			return executeBatched(ctx, p, plans, result)
190		}
191	
192		result.Pushed = len(plans)
193		return result, nil
194	}
195	
196	// hoistSourceHeadPlan moves the plan whose source ref matches the
197	// source's symref HEAD target to the front, so the resulting push
198	// commands send that ref first. Hosts that pick the default branch
199	// from the first push on a fresh repo (GitHub, GitLab) end up with the
200	// right default. Matching is on SourceRef rather than TargetRef so
201	// --map remappings push the correct (mapped) target ref first.
202	func hoistSourceHeadPlan(plans []planner.BranchPlan, sourceHEAD plumbing.ReferenceName) []planner.BranchPlan {
203		if sourceHEAD == "" {
204			return plans
205		}
206		out, _ := hoistFirstMatch(plans, func(p planner.BranchPlan) bool {
207			return p.SourceRef == sourceHEAD
208		})
209		return out
210	}
211	
212	// hoistFirstMatch moves the first element satisfying match to the front
213	// of xs. Returns the (possibly new) slice and the original index of the
214	// hoisted element (-1 when no match). When the match is already at
215	// position 0, returns the input slice unchanged.
216	func hoistFirstMatch[T any](xs []T, match func(T) bool) ([]T, int) {
217		for i, x := range xs {
218			if !match(x) {
219				continue
220			}
221			if i == 0 {
222				return xs, 0
223			}
224			out := make([]T, 0, len(xs))
225			out = append(out, x)
226			out = append(out, xs[:i]...)
227			out = append(out, xs[i+1:]...)
228			return out, i
229		}
230		return xs, -1
231	}
232	
233	func adjustedBootstrapTargetRefs(
234		desiredRefs map[plumbing.ReferenceName]planner.DesiredRef,
235		targetRefs map[plumbing.ReferenceName]plumbing.Hash,
236	) map[plumbing.ReferenceName]plumbing.Hash {
237		if len(targetRefs) == 0 {
238			return targetRefs
239		}
240		adjusted := planner.CopyRefHashMap(targetRefs)
241		for targetRef, desired := range desiredRefs {
242			if desired.Kind != planner.RefKindBranch {
243				continue
244			}
245			tempRef := planner.BootstrapTempRef(targetRef)
246			if adjusted[targetRef] == desired.SourceHash && adjusted[tempRef] == desired.SourceHash {
247				adjusted[targetRef] = plumbing.ZeroHash
248			}
249		}
250		return adjusted
251	}
252	
253	// --- Batched bootstrap ---
254	
255	func executeBatched( //nolint:maintidx // complex batch logic is inherently branchy
256		ctx context.Context,
257		p Params,
258		plans []planner.BranchPlan,
259		result Result,
260	) (Result, error) {
261		if !p.SourceService.SupportsBootstrapBatch() {
262			return result, errors.New("bootstrap batching requires protocol v2 source fetch filter support")
263		}
264	
265		// Tags and other-kind refs are create-only and ride a single tail phase
266		// after the checkpointed branch batches; they reuse branch-tip haves.
267		planRefs := make([]planner.DesiredRef, 0, len(plans))
268		tailPlans := make([]planner.BranchPlan, 0, len(plans))
269		for _, plan := range plans {
270			if plan.Kind == planner.RefKindTag || plan.Kind == planner.RefKindOther {
271				tailPlans = append(tailPlans, plan)
272				continue
273			}
274			if !plan.SourceRef.IsBranch() || !plan.TargetRef.IsBranch() {
275				return result, errors.New("bootstrap batching currently supports branch refs, tags, and other-kind create-only refs")
276			}
277			planRefs = append(planRefs, p.DesiredRefs[plan.TargetRef])
278		}
279		tailDesired := convert.DesiredRefsForPlans(p.DesiredRefs, tailPlans)
280	
281		var batches []plannedBatch
282		if len(planRefs) > 0 {
283			p.log("bootstrap batch planning checkpoints", "branch_ref_count", len(planRefs))
284			var err error
285			batches, err = planBatches(ctx, p, planRefs)
286			if err != nil {
287				return result, err
288			}
289		}
290	
291		// MaxPackBytes is the hard abort threshold for any single source fetch.
292		// TargetMaxPack controls checkpoint *placement* (how many batches) but
293		// should not cap individual fetches — the estimate may undercount, and
294		// the actual pack for a batch can legitimately exceed the planning
295		// heuristic. If the resulting pack is too large for the target's
296		// receive-pack, the push itself fails and resume handles retry.
297		fetchLimit := p.MaxPackBytes
298		// Track branch tips already pushed to the target so subsequent branches
299		// can advertise them as haves. Without this, the second branch in a
300		// multi-branch bootstrap re-sends all shared objects (e.g., linux's
301		// master and nocache-cleanup share ~99% of history).
302		completedRefs := planner.CopyRefHashMap(p.TargetRefs)
303	
304		// calibratedBytesPerObject tracks the per-object byte estimate
305		// updated from observed rejected pushes. Starts at the static
306		// default (which under-counts blob-heavy repos) and ratchets up as
307		// 413s reveal that the real bytes/object are much higher than 750.
308		// Used in checkPackSizeAndSubdivide so subsequent sub-pack fetches
309		// are pre-emptively split when the calibrated estimate exceeds
310		// p.TargetMaxPack — saving an entire ~limit-sized wasted upload.
311		calibratedBytesPerObject := int64(estimatedBytesPerObject)
312	
313		// selfImposedBudget tracks the byte ceiling we use for mid-stream
314		// abort. Initialised from the user-supplied target limit; ratchets
315		// down each time we observe a smaller server-side cutoff (parsed
316		// 413 limit or, more commonly with reverse proxies that don't
317		// announce the limit in the response, the bytes we managed to send
318		// before the connection was cut). Re-used across attempts so every
319		// failed push refines the budget.
320		selfImposedBudget := p.TargetMaxPack
321	
322		for _, batch := range batches {
323			if batch.subsumed {
324				cmds := []gitproto.PushCommand{{
325					Name: batch.Plan.TargetRef,
326					Old:  plumbing.ZeroHash,
327					New:  batch.Plan.SourceHash,
328				}}
329				if err := p.TargetPusher.PushCommands(ctx, cmds); err != nil {
330					return result, fmt.Errorf("create subsumed branch ref for %s: %w", batch.Plan.TargetRef, err)
331				}
332				completedRefs[batch.Plan.TargetRef] = batch.Plan.SourceHash
333				result.BatchCount++
334				p.log("bootstrap batch subsumed branch finalized",
335					"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
336					"source_hash", planner.ShortHash(batch.Plan.SourceHash))
337				continue
338			}
339			result.PlannedBatchCount += len(batch.Checkpoints)
340			result.TempRefs = append(result.TempRefs, batch.TempRef.String())
341			p.log("bootstrap batch branch plan",
342				"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
343				"temp_ref", batch.TempRef.String(),
344				"planned_batches", len(batch.Checkpoints),
345				"resume_hash", planner.ShortHash(batch.ResumeHash))
346	
347			current := batch.ResumeHash
348			startIdx, err := planner.BootstrapResumeIndex(batch.Checkpoints, batch.ResumeHash)
349			if err != nil && !batch.ResumeHash.IsZero() && len(batch.chain) > 0 {
350				// Temp ref doesn't match any planned checkpoint (e.g., the user
351				// changed --target-max-pack-bytes between runs). If the hash is
352				// in the commit chain, reuse it as the starting point and re-plan
353				// remaining checkpoints — preserving already-pushed data.
354				if chainIdx := chainPosition(batch.chain, batch.ResumeHash); chainIdx >= 0 {
355					remaining := batch.chain[chainIdx+1:]
356					if len(remaining) > 0 {
357						numBatches := estimateBatchCount(int64(len(remaining)), p.TargetMaxPack)
358						batch.Checkpoints = evenCheckpoints(remaining, numBatches)
359						p.log("bootstrap batch resuming from stale temp ref",
360							"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
361							"resume_hash", planner.ShortHash(batch.ResumeHash),
362							"remaining_commits", len(remaining),
363							"new_batches", len(batch.Checkpoints))
364						startIdx = 0
365						err = nil
366					}
367				}
368			}
369			if err != nil && !batch.ResumeHash.IsZero() {
370				// Temp ref hash not in the chain at all — truly stale. Delete and start fresh.
371				p.log("bootstrap batch clearing stale temp ref",
372					"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
373					"temp_ref", batch.TempRef.String(),
374					"stale_hash", planner.ShortHash(batch.ResumeHash))
375				delCmds := []gitproto.PushCommand{{Name: batch.TempRef, Old: batch.ResumeHash, Delete: true}}
376				if delErr := p.TargetPusher.PushCommands(ctx, delCmds); delErr != nil {
377					return result, fmt.Errorf("delete stale temp ref %s: %w (original: %w)", batch.TempRef, delErr, err)
378				}
379				current = plumbing.ZeroHash
380				startIdx = 0
381			}
382	
383			// Track every commit known to be in the target so the source
384			// can use them all as fetch haves. With topo ordering a merge's
385			// "delta" otherwise drags in the full ancestry of the non-first
386			// parent, even though we pushed those commits as their own
387			// checkpoints earlier in the chain (or in earlier runs).
388			//
389			// On resume, seed from the chain prefix at-or-before `current`:
390			// every prior topo iteration must have pushed those commits to
391			// advance the temp ref to its current position. Declaring just
392			// `current` is insufficient because in topo order the earlier
393			// chain entries aren't necessarily its ancestors — they can sit
394			// on side branches that only get merged later.
395			pushedCheckpoints := make([]plumbing.Hash, 0, len(batch.chain))
396			if !current.IsZero() && len(batch.chain) > 0 {
397				if idx := chainPosition(batch.chain, current); idx >= 0 {
398					pushedCheckpoints = append(pushedCheckpoints, batch.chain[:idx+1]...)
399				}
400			}
401	
402			// Manual index loop: subdivide may insert checkpoints at the current
403			// index, so we must not auto-increment after a retry.
404			idx := startIdx
405			for idx < len(batch.Checkpoints) {
406				checkpoint := batch.Checkpoints[idx]
407				if p.OnPhase != nil {
408					p.OnPhase(fmt.Sprintf("pack %d/%d", idx+1, len(batch.Checkpoints)))
409				}
410				p.log("bootstrap batch push checkpoint",
411					"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
412					"batch", idx+1,
413					"batch_total", len(batch.Checkpoints),
414					"from", planner.ShortHash(current),
415					"to", planner.ShortHash(checkpoint))
416	
417				stagePlans := []planner.BranchPlan{{
418					Branch: batch.Plan.Branch, SourceRef: batch.Plan.SourceRef,
419					TargetRef: batch.TempRef, SourceHash: checkpoint, TargetHash: current,
420					Kind: batch.Plan.Kind, Action: planner.ActionForTargetHash(current),
421					Reason: fmt.Sprintf("%s -> %s via %s", planner.ShortHash(current), planner.ShortHash(checkpoint), batch.TempRef),
422				}}
423				if idx == len(batch.Checkpoints)-1 {
424					stagePlans = append(stagePlans, planner.BranchPlan{
425						Branch: batch.Plan.Branch, SourceRef: batch.Plan.SourceRef,
426						TargetRef: batch.Plan.TargetRef, SourceHash: checkpoint,
427						TargetHash: plumbing.ZeroHash, Kind: batch.Plan.Kind,
428						Action: planner.ActionCreate,
429						Reason: fmt.Sprintf("create %s at %s", batch.Plan.TargetRef, planner.ShortHash(checkpoint)),
430					})
431				}
432	
433				packReader, err := packReaderForCheckpoint(ctx, p, batch, checkpoint, pushedCheckpoints, completedRefs, fetchLimit)
434				if err != nil {
435					return result, fmt.Errorf("fetch source batch pack for %s: %w", batch.Plan.TargetRef, err)
436				}
437				packReader = closeOnce(packReader)
438	
439				// Peek at the PACK header (12 bytes) to get the object count.
440				// If the estimated pack size (objectCount × calibrated
441				// bytesPerObject) exceeds the batch limit, subdivide
442				// immediately instead of pushing a pack the target will reject.
443				// This avoids wasting a multi-GiB transfer on a doomed push.
444				var packObjectCount int64
445				if p.TargetMaxPack > 0 && len(batch.chain) > 0 {
446					subdivided := false
447					packReader, packObjectCount, err = checkPackSizeAndSubdivide(packReader, p.TargetMaxPack, calibratedBytesPerObject, func(estimated int64) bool {
448						expanded := subdivideCheckpoints(batch.chain, current, batch.Checkpoints[idx:])
449						if len(expanded) > len(batch.Checkpoints[idx:]) {
450							oldRemaining := len(batch.Checkpoints[idx:])
451							newCount := len(expanded)
452							perPack := estimated / int64(newCount)
453							p.log("bootstrap batch subdividing before push (pack header estimate)",
454								"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
455								"old_remaining", oldRemaining,
456								"new_remaining", newCount,
457								"estimated_bytes", estimated,
458								"calibrated_bytes_per_object", calibratedBytesPerObject)
459							p.notice(fmt.Sprintf(
460								"estimated pack ~%s exceeds target limit %s — splitting %d → %d packs (~%s each)",
461								humanBytes(estimated), humanBytes(p.TargetMaxPack),
462								oldRemaining, newCount, humanBytes(perPack),
463							))
464							batch.Checkpoints = append(batch.Checkpoints[:idx], expanded...)
465							subdivided = true
466							return true
467						}
468						return false
469					})
470					if err != nil {
471						return result, fmt.Errorf("check pack size for %s: %w", batch.Plan.TargetRef, err)
472					}
473					if subdivided {
474						continue // retry at same idx with new (smaller) checkpoint
475					}
476				}
477				p.log("bootstrap batch push attempting",
478					"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
479					"batch", idx+1,
480					"batch_total", len(batch.Checkpoints),
481					"estimated_bytes", packObjectCount*calibratedBytesPerObject,
482					"object_count", packObjectCount,
483					"target_limit_bytes", p.TargetMaxPack,
484					"calibrated_bytes_per_object", calibratedBytesPerObject)
485	
486				cmds := convert.PlansToPushCommands(stagePlans, false)
487				observer := newPackStreamObserver(packReader)
488				if selfImposedBudget > 0 {
489					budget := selfImposedBudget
490					observer.SetAborter(func(bytesSent, objectsSent, totalObjects int64) bool {
491						return shouldAbortPush(bytesSent, objectsSent, totalObjects, budget)
492					})
493				}
494				pushErr := p.TargetPusher.PushPack(ctx, cmds, observer)
495				sentBytes := observer.Bytes()
496				objectsSent := observer.ObjectsSent()
497				totalObjects := observer.TotalObjects()
498				abortedEarly := observer.Aborted()
499				if pushErr != nil {
500					_ = packReader.Close()
501					// Treat abortedEarly the same as a body-limit error:
502					// both indicate "this pack is too big for the target",
503					// just one is detected by the server and one by us.
504					sizeIssue := abortedEarly || isTargetBodyLimitError(pushErr)
505					p.log("bootstrap batch push failed",
506						"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
507						"batch", idx+1,
508						"batch_total", len(batch.Checkpoints),
509						"estimated_bytes", packObjectCount*calibratedBytesPerObject,
510						"target_limit_bytes", p.TargetMaxPack,
511						"sent_bytes", sentBytes,
512						"object_count", packObjectCount,
513						"objects_sent", objectsSent,
514						"total_objects_in_pack", totalObjects,
515						"aborted_early", abortedEarly,
516						"will_subdivide", sizeIssue && len(batch.chain) > 0,
517						"error", pushErr.Error())
518					if sizeIssue && len(batch.chain) > 0 {
519						parsedLimit := targetBodyLimit(pushErr)
520						limit := p.TargetMaxPack
521						if parsedLimit > 0 {
522							limit = parsedLimit
523						} else if abortedEarly && selfImposedBudget > 0 {
524							limit = selfImposedBudget
525						}
526						// Calibrate before subdividing. The new value carries
527						// over to the next iteration's pre-flight check, so a
528						// blob-heavy repo's sub-packs get caught earlier.
529						//
530						// When we have an objectsSent observation from the
531						// streaming parser, divide by THAT rather than the
532						// full pack header count. sentBytes covers exactly
533						// objectsSent objects (the front of the pack), so
534						// sentBytes/objectsSent is the accurate per-object
535						// average for the portion we actually observed —
536						// and for blob-front-loaded repos that's a pessimistic
537						// upper bound, which is what we want for pre-flight.
538						//
539						// When the header was parsed but no object completed
540						// (a single front-loaded large blob exhausted the
541						// abort floor), treat it as one observed object: we
542						// know that first object alone consumed sentBytes,
543						// which is the right pessimistic per-object input.
544						effObjectsSent := effectiveObjectsSent(objectsSent, totalObjects, abortedEarly)
545						calibrationDenom := packObjectCount
546						if effObjectsSent > 0 && effObjectsSent < calibrationDenom {
547							calibrationDenom = effObjectsSent
548						}
549						if updated := calibrateBytesPerObject(sentBytes, calibrationDenom, calibratedBytesPerObject); updated > 0 {
550							p.log("bootstrap batch calibrated bytes-per-object",
551								"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
552								"previous_bytes_per_object", calibratedBytesPerObject,
553								"observed_bytes_per_object", updated,
554								"sent_bytes", sentBytes,
555								"calibration_denom", calibrationDenom,
556								"object_count", packObjectCount,
557								"objects_sent", objectsSent)
558							calibratedBytesPerObject = updated
559						}
560						selfImposedBudget = nextSelfImposedBudget(selfImposedBudget, parsedLimit, sentBytes, abortedEarly)
561						// Pick the byte count we use for sizing the next
562						// subdivision. When the server cut us off, sentBytes
563						// is roughly the cap and using it directly is right.
564						// When *we* cut the upload early, sentBytes is just
565						// the abort point (~minBytesBeforeAbort) — much less
566						// than the real pack size — so the factor would
567						// converge slowly. Project from observed bytes/object
568						// to the full pack size when we have the data, so
569						// factor reflects the real overshoot.
570						sizingBytes := sentBytes
571						if abortedEarly && totalObjects > 0 && effObjectsSent > 0 {
572							if projected := sentBytes * totalObjects / effObjectsSent; projected > sizingBytes {
573								sizingBytes = projected
574							}
575						}
576						factor := observedSubdivisionFactor(sizingBytes, limit)
577						expanded := subdivideToFactor(batch.chain, current, batch.Checkpoints[idx:], factor)
578						if len(expanded) > len(batch.Checkpoints[idx:]) {
579							oldRemaining := len(batch.Checkpoints[idx:])
580							newCount := len(expanded)
581							p.log("bootstrap batch subdividing after target size rejection",
582								"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
583								"old_remaining", oldRemaining,
584								"new_remaining", newCount,
585								"sent_bytes", sentBytes,
586								"sizing_bytes", sizingBytes,
587								"limit_bytes", limit,
588								"factor", factor,
589								"aborted_early", abortedEarly,
590								"error", pushErr.Error())
591							limitText := ""
592							if limit > 0 {
593								limitText = fmt.Sprintf(" (target limit %s)", humanBytes(limit))
594							}
595							reason := "target rejected pack"
596							if abortedEarly {
597								reason = "projected to exceed target limit"
598							}
599							p.notice(fmt.Sprintf("%s%s — splitting %d → %d packs",
600								reason, limitText, oldRemaining, newCount))
601							batch.Checkpoints = append(batch.Checkpoints[:idx], expanded...)
602							continue // retry at same idx with new (smaller) checkpoint
603						}
604					}
605					return result, fmt.Errorf("push bootstrap batch for %s: %w", batch.Plan.TargetRef, pushErr)
606				}
607				_ = packReader.Close()
608				p.log("bootstrap batch checkpoint complete",
609					"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
610					"batch", idx+1,
611					"batch_total", len(batch.Checkpoints))
612				current = checkpoint
613				pushedCheckpoints = append(pushedCheckpoints, checkpoint)
614				result.BatchCount++
615	
616				// Recombine: subdivision is a one-way ratchet, so the fine
617				// granularity needed for one heavy commit sticks around for
618				// the rest of the chain even when the deltas afterward are
619				// tiny. Aim for the next pack to be roughly half the target
620				// limit by dropping enough checkpoints that the span doubles
621				// approximately log2(target/2 / sent) times. If we
622				// overshoot, the abort-early + subdivision path re-splits.
623				// Leave at least the final checkpoint after idx (it carries
624				// the SourceHash cutover), so the cap is len-idx-2.
625				if dropCount := recombineDropCount(sentBytes, p.TargetMaxPack, len(batch.Checkpoints)-idx-2); dropCount > 0 {
626					dropped := batch.Checkpoints[idx+1]
627					batch.Checkpoints = append(batch.Checkpoints[:idx+1], batch.Checkpoints[idx+1+dropCount:]...)
628					p.log("bootstrap batch recombining after small push",
629						"branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String(),
630						"sent_bytes", sentBytes,
631						"target_limit_bytes", p.TargetMaxPack,
632						"dropped_count", dropCount,
633						"first_dropped_checkpoint", planner.ShortHash(dropped),
634						"remaining_checkpoints", len(batch.Checkpoints))
635				}
636				idx++
637			}
638	
639			if current.IsZero() {
640				return result, fmt.Errorf("bootstrap batching for %s completed with no checkpoint state", batch.Plan.TargetRef)
641			}
642			if batch.ResumeHash == batch.Plan.SourceHash && p.TargetRefs[batch.Plan.TargetRef].IsZero() {
643				cmds := []gitproto.PushCommand{{Name: batch.Plan.TargetRef, Old: plumbing.ZeroHash, New: batch.Plan.SourceHash}}
644				if err := p.TargetPusher.PushCommands(ctx, cmds); err != nil {
645					return result, fmt.Errorf("resume bootstrap cutover for %s: %w", batch.Plan.TargetRef, err)
646				}
647			}
648	
649			cmds := []gitproto.PushCommand{{Name: batch.TempRef, Old: current, Delete: true}}
650			if err := p.TargetPusher.PushCommands(ctx, cmds); err != nil {
651				return result, fmt.Errorf("delete bootstrap temp ref for %s: %w", batch.Plan.TargetRef, err)
652			}
653			completedRefs[batch.Plan.TargetRef] = batch.Plan.SourceHash
654			completedRefs[batch.TempRef] = batch.Plan.SourceHash
655			p.log("bootstrap batch branch finalized", "branch", batch.Plan.TargetRef.String())
656		}
657	
658		// Tail phase: tags and other-kind refs (issue #1)
659		if len(tailPlans) > 0 {
660			p.log("bootstrap batch pushing tail refs after branch batches", "tail_count", len(tailPlans))
661			if p.OnPhase != nil {
662				p.OnPhase(tailPhaseLabel(tailPlans))
663			}
664			tailTargetRefs := planner.CopyRefHashMap(p.TargetRefs)
665			for _, batch := range batches {
666				tailTargetRefs[batch.Plan.TargetRef] = batch.Plan.SourceHash
667			}
668			packReader, err := p.SourceService.FetchPack(ctx, p.SourceConn, tailDesired, tailTargetRefs)
669			if err != nil {
670				if errors.Is(err, git.NoErrAlreadyUpToDate) {
671					cmds := convert.PlansToPushCommands(tailPlans, false)
672					if err := p.TargetPusher.PushCommands(ctx, cmds); err != nil {
673						return result, fmt.Errorf("create tail refs after bootstrap: %w", err)
674					}
675				} else {
676					return result, fmt.Errorf("fetch bootstrap tail pack: %w", err)
677				}
678			} else {
679				packReader = gitproto.LimitPackReader(packReader, p.MaxPackBytes)
680				packReader = closeOnce(packReader)
681				cmds := convert.PlansToPushCommands(tailPlans, false)
682				if err := p.TargetPusher.PushPack(ctx, cmds, packReader); err != nil {
683					_ = packReader.Close()
684					return result, fmt.Errorf("push bootstrap tail refs: %w", err)
685				}
686				_ = packReader.Close()
687			}
688		}
689	
690		result.Pushed = len(plans)
691		result.Batching = true
692		result.RelayMode = "bootstrap-batch"
693		return result, nil
694	}
695	
696	// tailPhaseLabel returns a phase label matching what's in plans.
697	func tailPhaseLabel(plans []planner.BranchPlan) string {
698		hasTag, hasOther := false, false
699		for _, plan := range plans {
700			switch plan.Kind {
701			case planner.RefKindTag:
702				hasTag = true
703			case planner.RefKindOther:
704				hasOther = true
705			case planner.RefKindBranch:
706			}
707		}
708		switch {
709		case hasTag && hasOther:
710			return "pushing tail refs"
711		case hasOther:
712			return "pushing other refs"
713		default:
714			return "pushing tags"
715		}
716	}
717	
718	// --- Checkpoint planning ---
719	
720	func planBatches(ctx context.Context, p Params, desired []planner.DesiredRef) ([]plannedBatch, error) {
721		ordered, trunkIdx := orderTrunkFirst(desired, p.SourceHeadTarget)
722		switch {
723		case p.SourceHeadTarget == "":
724			p.log("bootstrap batch trunk unset", "reason", "source did not advertise a HEAD symref")
725		case trunkIdx < 0:
726			p.log("bootstrap batch trunk unset",
727				"reason", "HEAD ref not in desired set (filtered by --branch or --map)",
728				"source_head_target", p.SourceHeadTarget.String())
729		default:
730			p.log("bootstrap batch trunk selected",
731				"source_head_target", p.SourceHeadTarget.String(),
732				"trunk_target_ref", ordered[trunkIdx].TargetRef.String())
733		}
734		out := make([]plannedBatch, 0, len(ordered))
735	
736		var (
737			trunkStopSet map[plumbing.Hash]struct{}
738			trunkHaves   []plumbing.Hash
739		)
740	
741		for i, ref := range ordered {
742			// Branches whose tip is already reachable from trunk's ancestry need
743			// no pack transfer — trunk's batches already delivered every object.
744			// Emit a subsumed batch that the executor handles with a single ref
745			// create command.
746			if i != trunkIdx && trunkStopSet != nil {
747				if _, subsumed := trunkStopSet[ref.SourceHash]; subsumed {
748					p.log("bootstrap batch branch subsumed by trunk",
749						"branch", ref.TargetRef.String(),
750						"source_hash", planner.ShortHash(ref.SourceHash))
751					out = append(out, plannedBatch{
752						BootstrapBatch: planner.BootstrapBatch{
753							Plan: planner.BranchPlan{
754								Branch: ref.Label, SourceRef: ref.SourceRef,
755								TargetRef: ref.TargetRef, SourceHash: ref.SourceHash,
756								Kind: ref.Kind, Action: planner.ActionCreate,
757							},
758						},
759						subsumed: true,
760					})
761					continue
762				}
763			}
764			var (
765				haves  []plumbing.Hash
766				stopAt map[plumbing.Hash]struct{}
767			)
768			if i != trunkIdx && trunkStopSet != nil {
769				haves = trunkHaves
770				stopAt = trunkStopSet
771			}
772			checkpoints, chain, ancestors, err := planCheckpointsFromChain(ctx, p, ref, haves, stopAt)
773			if err != nil {
774				return nil, err
775			}
776			if i == trunkIdx && trunkIdx >= 0 {
777				trunkStopSet = ancestors
778				trunkHaves = []plumbing.Hash{ref.SourceHash}
779			}
780			out = append(out, plannedBatch{
781				BootstrapBatch: planner.BootstrapBatch{
782					Plan: planner.BranchPlan{
783						Branch: ref.Label, SourceRef: ref.SourceRef,
784						TargetRef: ref.TargetRef, SourceHash: ref.SourceHash,
785						Kind: ref.Kind, Action: planner.ActionCreate,
786					},
787					TempRef:     planner.BootstrapTempRef(ref.TargetRef),
788					ResumeHash:  p.TargetRefs[planner.BootstrapTempRef(ref.TargetRef)],
789					Checkpoints: checkpoints,
790				},
791				chain: chain,
792			})
793		}
794		return out, nil
795	}
796	
797	// orderTrunkFirst returns desired reordered so the branch matching
798	// sourceHeadTarget is first. If no match, the original order is preserved and
799	// trunkIdx is -1. Otherwise trunkIdx is 0 (the position of the trunk).
800	func orderTrunkFirst(desired []planner.DesiredRef, sourceHeadTarget plumbing.ReferenceName) ([]planner.DesiredRef, int) {
801		if sourceHeadTarget == "" {
802			return desired, -1
803		}
804		reordered, idx := hoistFirstMatch(desired, func(r planner.DesiredRef) bool {
805			return r.SourceRef == sourceHeadTarget
806		})
807		if idx < 0 {
808			return desired, -1
809		}
810		return reordered, 0
811	}
812	
813	// PlanCheckpoints plans the checkpoint hashes for a single branch during batched bootstrap.
814	func PlanCheckpoints(ctx context.Context, p Params, ref planner.DesiredRef) ([]plumbing.Hash, error) {
815		checkpoints, _, _, err := planCheckpointsFromChain(ctx, p, ref, nil, nil)
816		return checkpoints, err
817	}
818	
819	// estimatedBytesPerCommit is the heuristic for estimating pack size from commit
820	// count. Real repos range from ~5 KiB/commit (small web apps) to ~120 KiB
821	// (blob-heavy monorepos); most mature repos fall in 20–80 KiB. 64 KiB produces
822	// accurate batch counts for large repos (linux is ~66 KiB/commit) while
823	// slightly overestimating for small ones (harmless — extra batches finish fast).
824	// The PACK header pre-check and target-rejection retry catch remaining error.
825	const estimatedBytesPerCommit = 65536
826	
827	// planCheckpointsFromChain fetches the source-side commit graph for a branch
828	// and derives checkpoint hashes for batched bootstrap. When trunkStopAt is
829	// provided, the walk terminates at commits already reachable from the trunk,
830	// and trunkHaves is passed to the source fetch so shared history is not
831	// resent. The returned ancestors set covers every commit in the fetched graph
832	// (not just the first-parent chain), so callers can accumulate it as a
833	// cutoff for subsequent branches.
834	func planCheckpointsFromChain(
835		ctx context.Context,
836		p Params,
837		ref planner.DesiredRef,
838		trunkHaves []plumbing.Hash,
839		trunkStopAt map[plumbing.Hash]struct{},
840	) ([]plumbing.Hash, []plumbing.Hash, map[plumbing.Hash]struct{}, error) {
841		p.log("bootstrap batch fetching commit graph",
842			"branch", ref.TargetRef.String(),
843			"have_count", len(trunkHaves),
844			"stop_at_count", len(trunkStopAt))
845	
846		// Stream the tree:0-filtered commit graph and extract (commit ->
847		// parent hashes) directly from the pack, discarding all object
848		// content as it arrives. For linux this drops the planning-phase
849		// peak from ~4.6 GiB (full in-memory store of ~1.4M decoded
850		// commits) to ~100 MiB (parents map plus a small fixed delta
851		// cache). See internal/gitproto.ExtractCommitParents. With trunk
852		// haves the source sends only divergent commits, so feature
853		// branches' planning is already small either way.
854		gpRef := gitproto.DesiredRef{SourceRef: ref.SourceRef, TargetRef: ref.TargetRef, SourceHash: ref.SourceHash}
855		parentsMap, err := p.SourceService.FetchCommitParents(ctx, p.SourceConn, gpRef, trunkHaves)
856		if err != nil {
857			return nil, nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("fetch bootstrap commit parents for %s: %w", ref.TargetRef, err)
858		}
859		var chain []plumbing.Hash
860		switch p.Strategy {
861		case "", "first-parent":
862			c, walkErr := planner.FirstParentChainFromParents(parentsMap, ref.SourceHash, trunkStopAt)
863			if walkErr != nil {
864				return nil, nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("walk first-parent chain for %s: %w", ref.TargetRef, walkErr)
865			}
866			chain = c
867		case "topo":
868			c, walkErr := planner.TopoChainFromParents(parentsMap, ref.SourceHash, trunkStopAt)
869			if walkErr != nil {
870				return nil, nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("walk topo chain for %s: %w", ref.TargetRef, walkErr)
871			}
872			chain = c
873		default:
874			return nil, nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("unsupported bootstrap strategy %q (want \"first-parent\" or \"topo\")", p.Strategy)
875		}
876		// Every commit the source sent is now an ancestor we can use as a
877		// stop set for subsequent branches. Map keys give us the set
878		// directly.
879		ancestors := make(map[plumbing.Hash]struct{}, len(parentsMap))
880		for h := range parentsMap {
881			ancestors[h] = struct{}{}
882		}
883	
884		if len(chain) == 0 {
885			// Tip is already covered by the stop set. Emit a single-checkpoint
886			// batch so downstream push logic still creates the target ref; the
887			// accompanying fetch will find nothing new via haves.
888			chain = []plumbing.Hash{ref.SourceHash}
889		}
890	
891		numBatches := estimateBatchCount(int64(len(chain)), p.TargetMaxPack)
892		checkpoints := evenCheckpoints(chain, numBatches)
893	
894		p.log("bootstrap batch planned checkpoints",
895			"branch", ref.TargetRef.String(),
896			"chain_len", len(chain),
897			"estimated_batches", len(checkpoints))
898	
899		return checkpoints, chain, ancestors, nil
900	}
901	
902	func estimateBatchCount(chainLen int64, batchMaxPack int64) int {
903		if batchMaxPack <= 0 || chainLen <= 0 {
904			return 1
905		}
906		estimated := chainLen * estimatedBytesPerCommit
907		n := int((estimated + batchMaxPack - 1) / batchMaxPack)
908		return max(n, 1)
909	}
910	
911	// estimatedBytesPerObject is a conservative average for compressed git objects
912	// in a packfile. Used with the PACK header's object count to estimate total
913	// pack size before streaming the full pack. Real values range from ~200 bytes
914	// (tiny commits in a sparse repo) to ~2 KiB (blob-heavy repos), with most
915	// mature repos averaging 500–1000 bytes. 750 is a reasonable middle ground.
916	const estimatedBytesPerObject = 750
917	
918	// checkPackSizeAndSubdivide reads the 12-byte PACK header, multiplies
919	// the object count by bytesPerObject to estimate total pack size, and
920	// subdivides via the callback when the estimate exceeds batchLimit.
921	// Returns (nil, objectCount, nil) when subdivided (caller should retry
922	// at the same idx), (prepended reader, objectCount, nil) to proceed
923	// with push, or (prepended reader, 0, nil) when the header could not
924	// be parsed.
925	//
926	// bytesPerObject lets the caller use a per-run calibrated value
927	// instead of the static estimatedBytesPerObject default. Calibrating
928	// after each rejection (using the bytes that flowed through
929	// packStreamObserver) catches blob-heavy repos where the static 750-byte
930	// average is 10–20× too low — without calibration the pre-flight
931	// would let oversized sub-packs through and the loop would only learn
932	// after another wasted ~limit-sized upload. The subdivide callback
933	// receives the estimated total bytes so user-facing messages can
934	// quote the projected size that triggered the split.
935	func checkPackSizeAndSubdivide(
936		r io.ReadCloser,
937		batchLimit int64,
938		bytesPerObject int64,
939		subdivide func(estimatedBytes int64) bool,
940	) (io.ReadCloser, int64, error) { //nolint:unparam // error return kept for future use
941		if bytesPerObject <= 0 {
942			bytesPerObject = estimatedBytesPerObject
943		}
944		var header [12]byte
945		n, err := io.ReadFull(r, header[:])
946		if err != nil {
947			// Short pack or error — let the push handle it
948			prefixed := io.MultiReader(bytes.NewReader(header[:n]), r)
949			return &wrappedMultiRC{Reader: prefixed, Closer: r}, 0, nil //nolint:nilerr // below threshold is not an error, we return the original reader
950		}
951		if string(header[:4]) != "PACK" {
952			// Not a standard packfile — can't estimate, proceed
953			prefixed := io.MultiReader(bytes.NewReader(header[:]), r)
954			return &wrappedMultiRC{Reader: prefixed, Closer: r}, 0, nil
955		}
956		objectCount := int64(header[8])<<24 | int64(header[9])<<16 | int64(header[10])<<8 | int64(header[11])
957		estimated := objectCount * bytesPerObject
958	
959		if estimated > batchLimit && subdivide(estimated) {
960			_ = r.Close()
961			return nil, objectCount, nil
962		}
963	
964		prefixed := io.MultiReader(bytes.NewReader(header[:]), r)
965		return &wrappedMultiRC{Reader: prefixed, Closer: r}, objectCount, nil
966	}
967	
968	// calibrateBytesPerObject derives a per-object byte estimate from a
969	// rejected push's transmitted-bytes lower bound. sentBytes is the
970	// amount our request-body counter saw before the server cut us off,
971	// which means the true pack size is at least sentBytes — likely more.
972	// The 2× safety multiplier projects from observed lower bound to a

content.text:
I've dug through the issue and the relevant code. Here's my read.

## What's happening

The user is doing a one-shot bootstrap relay from a GitLab source to a fresh GitHub target. git-sync streams the pack straight from the source's `upload-pack` into the target's `receive-pack` POST (`bootstrap.Execute` → `PushPack` → `sendReceivePack`, which builds an `io.MultiReader(header, packData)` and POSTs it at `internal/gitproto/push.go:301`).

GitHub returns **HTTP 408 (Request Timeout)** on `git-receive-pack`. GitHub enforces a wall-clock deadline on the receive-pack POST. The repo is large (compiled kernels/modules = big blobs), and the relay is bottlenecked by a very slow source — the progress line shows `gitlab.baylibre.com → 4.59 KB @ 402 B/s` feeding `479 B @ 39 B/s → github.com`. Because it's a direct relay, the slow GitLab read rate-limits the GitHub write, the single giant POST runs past GitHub's deadline, and GitHub kills it with 408. So the user's instinct in the issue is right: repo size (combined with slow source throughput) is the cause.

## Why git-sync doesn't auto-recover here

There's already good machinery for "pack too big for the target" — batched bootstrap with checkpointing and auto-subdivision. But none of it triggers for this case:

1. **GitHub preflight** (`githubBatchLimit`, `bootstrap.go:1304`) only fires when the **source** is GitHub — it queries the GitHub API for the *source* repo's size. Here the source is GitLab, so it returns `false` and never pre-batches.
2. **Auto-batch-on-rejection** (`autoTargetMaxPackBytes`, `bootstrap.go:1380`) only fires for body-limit errors — `isTargetBodyLimitError` (`bootstrap.go:1430`) matches `413` / "body exceeded size limit" / "payload too large". **It does not match 408.**

So the 408 falls through `Execute`'s one-shot path (`bootstrap.go:179-190`) as a hard, unactionable failure. The error message also doesn't hint at `--target-max-pack-bytes`, which is the exact knob that would fix it (smaller packs each finish within GitHub's timeout window).

## What I'd recommend

**Immediate workaround for the user** (can post as a comment): pass `--target-max-pack-bytes` to force batched bootstrap, e.g. `--target-max-pack-bytes 104857600` (100 MiB). Batching also gives resumability across runs, which matters a lot for a slow link.

**Code fix** — treat a timeout-class rejection like a "this pack is too big for this target" signal so it benefits from the same batching:
- Classify `408` (and arguably gateway timeouts `502/503/504`) as a "split it up" condition feeding `autoTargetMaxPackBytes`, so a one-shot 408 retries in batched mode instead of hard-failing.
- When batching isn't possible (e.g. source lacks protocol-v2 filter support, so `SupportsBootstrapBatch()` is false), surface an actionable error pointing at `--target-max-pack-bytes`.

I'd keep 408 conceptually separate from the 413 size errors (name it something like a "target push deadline / retryable" class) even though it routes into the same batching remedy, since the trigger is a timeout, not a size rejection.

Want me to:
1. **Implement the fix** (classify 408/timeouts → batched retry + actionable error message), with tests, on a branch; or
2. **Post a diagnostic comment** on issue #70 with the workaround and root cause; or
3. both?

`/simplify → 4 cleanup agents in parallel → apply the fixes`

You are improving the quality of the changed code, not hunting for bugs. Review
it for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude issues, then fix what you
find. Do not look for correctness bugs — that is what `/code-review` is for.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Review (4 cleanup agents in parallel)

Launch **4 independent review agents** via the Agent tool, all in a
single message so they run concurrently. Pass each agent the diff and one of
the four angles below. Each returns its findings with `file`, `line`, a
one-line `summary`, and the concrete cost (what is duplicated, wasted, or
harder to maintain).

### Reuse

Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

## Phase 2 — Apply the fixes

Wait for all four agents to complete, dedup findings that point at the same
line or mechanism, and fix each remaining one directly. Skip any finding whose
fix would change intended behavior, require changes well outside the reviewed
diff, or that you judge to be a false positive — note the skip rather than
arguing with it. Finish with a brief summary of what was fixed and what was
skipped (or confirm the code was already clean).

This confirms the picture. The gitproto layer (`httpError`) does not classify any status codes itself — it just formats `http %d: ...`. All HTTP-status classification (413, and now 408/504) lives in the bootstrap package as substring matches on the formatted string. Meanwhile, the *report-status* "ng" rejection path got the full typed-error treatment (`RefRejectedError` / `ErrTargetRefMoved`) recently.

I have everything needed to give the recommendation.

## Altitude review: 408/504 "batchable push" classification

### Finding 1 — Substring-matching `"http 408"` on the formatted error string is at the wrong altitude, but it matches the package's *existing* HTTP-classification pattern

`internal/strategy/bootstrap/bootstrap.go:1448` (`isTargetPushDeadlineError`) classifies an HTTP status by `strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(err.Error()), "http 408")`. The status code is *known structurally* at `internal/gitproto/smarthttp.go:39` (`httpError` has `res.StatusCode`), then thrown away into a `fmt.Errorf("http %d: ...")` string at smarthttp.go:61/63, and re-parsed downstream.

- One-line summary: HTTP status re-derived by substring-scanning a formatted string instead of inspecting a typed error from the layer that has the status code.
- Concrete cost: brittle coupling to the exact format string `"http %d"`. Anyone reformatting httpError (e.g. "HTTP 408", "status 408", localizing, or adding the numeric code elsewhere in the URL/body — a body containing "...http 408..." would false-positive) silently breaks classification with no compile-time signal. The redacted URL or the echoed server body is already concatenated into the same string being scanned, so a 200 response whose body quotes "http 408" would misclassify.

### Finding 2 — There IS an established typed-error pattern in this exact area, and the new code does not follow it

The recent commits (`RefRejectedError`, `ErrTargetRefMoved` at `internal/gitproto/push.go:152-196`, re-exported at `errors.go:20-26`) deliberately moved *receive-pack rejection* classification away from string matching to `errors.As`/`errors.Is`. The doc comment at push.go:258-263 and errors.go:18-19 explicitly say to prefer this "over inspecting the error message text, which is free-form and server-specific." The ideal version of this change would add an `HTTPStatusError` (or sentinel `ErrTargetReceiveTimeout`) in gitproto, set in `httpError`, and have bootstrap test it via `errors.As`/`errors.Is` — directly analogous to the just-landed work.

- One-line summary: a typed/sentinel HTTP-error pattern was just established one package over; the new 408/504 code uses the older string-matching approach it was meant to replace.
- Concrete cost: the codebase now has two coexisting conventions for the same concern (classifying target push failures), making the string-matching path look blessed and inviting future copies.

### Finding 3 — But Finding 1/2 are NOT in scope for this diff; the string-match is locally consistent

`isTargetBodyLimitError` (bootstrap.go:1437) already establishes substring-matching `"http 413"` / `"body exceeded size limit"` as *this package's* accepted pattern, and `httpError` does not classify any status today. The new `isTargetPushDeadlineError` sits right beside it, mirrors its shape exactly, and `isBatchableTargetPushError` cleanly OR-composes the two. Re-architecting to typed errors would mean: a new error type in gitproto, threading the status through both `httpError` call sites (smarthttp.go:395, :499), re-exporting on the public API, and rewriting *both* the 413 and 408 detectors plus their tests — i.e. refactoring pre-existing code well beyond the timeout fix.

- Recommendation: ship as-is. The string-match is a pre-existing-pattern decision, not a regression this diff introduces; raising the altitude is a separate, larger cleanup. If anything, file a follow-up to migrate HTTP-status classification (413 + 408/504 together) into a gitproto typed error, mirroring the `RefRejectedError` work — but that does not belong in this small fix.

### Finding 4 — Conflating 408/504 (timeout) with 413 (size) under `isBatchableTargetPushError` is the right generalization, not a leaky bucket

The union is justified by what the *consumer* does with it, not by the failures being identical. Both feed exactly one decision: "subdivide into smaller packs and retry batched." The diff is careful here — it keeps `isTargetPushDeadlineError` separate from `isTargetBodyLimitError` (so size-limit *parsing*, `targetBodyLimit` at the 413 path, stays unpolluted), names the union by the *remedy* ("batchable") rather than the cause, and the comment at bootstrap.go:507-510 states the shared remedy explicitly. The only mild leak: a 408 is sometimes a transient slow-link hiccup rather than a too-big pack, so subdividing is a heuristic that can over-split a pack that wasn't actually oversized — but that degrades to "more, smaller, successful pushes" plus the recombine logic (bootstrap.go:632), which is acceptable and self-correcting.

- One-line summary: the union is keyed on shared remedy (subdivide-and-retry), with size-specific parsing kept on its own path — a sound abstraction boundary.

content.text:
This confirms the picture. The gitproto layer (`httpError`) does not classify any status codes itself — it just formats `http %d: ...`. All HTTP-status classification (413, and now 408/504) lives in the bootstrap package as substring matches on the formatted string. Meanwhile, the *report-status* "ng" rejection path got the full typed-error treatment (`RefRejectedError` / `ErrTargetRefMoved`) recently.

I have everything needed to give the recommendation.

## Altitude review: 408/504 "batchable push" classification

### Finding 1 — Substring-matching `"http 408"` on the formatted error string is at the wrong altitude, but it matches the package's *existing* HTTP-classification pattern

`internal/strategy/bootstrap/bootstrap.go:1448` (`isTargetPushDeadlineError`) classifies an HTTP status by `strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(err.Error()), "http 408")`. The status code is *known structurally* at `internal/gitproto/smarthttp.go:39` (`httpError` has `res.StatusCode`), then thrown away into a `fmt.Errorf("http %d: ...")` string at smarthttp.go:61/63, and re-parsed downstream.

- One-line summary: HTTP status re-derived by substring-scanning a formatted string instead of inspecting a typed error from the layer that has the status code.
- Concrete cost: brittle coupling to the exact format string `"http %d"`. Anyone reformatting httpError (e.g. "HTTP 408", "status 408", localizing, or adding the numeric code elsewhere in the URL/body — a body containing "...http 408..." would false-positive) silently breaks classification with no compile-time signal. The redacted URL or the echoed server body is already concatenated into the same string being scanned, so a 200 response whose body quotes "http 408" would misclassify.

### Finding 2 — There IS an established typed-error pattern in this exact area, and the new code does not follow it

The recent commits (`RefRejectedError`, `ErrTargetRefMoved` at `internal/gitproto/push.go:152-196`, re-exported at `errors.go:20-26`) deliberately moved *receive-pack rejection* classification away from string matching to `errors.As`/`errors.Is`. The doc comment at push.go:258-263 and errors.go:18-19 explicitly say to prefer this "over inspecting the error message text, which is free-form and server-specific." The ideal version of this change would add an `HTTPStatusError` (or sentinel `ErrTargetReceiveTimeout`) in gitproto, set in `httpError`, and have bootstrap test it via `errors.As`/`errors.Is` — directly analogous to the just-landed work.

- One-line summary: a typed/sentinel HTTP-error pattern was just established one package over; the new 408/504 code uses the older string-matching approach it was meant to replace.
- Concrete cost: the codebase now has two coexisting conventions for the same concern (classifying target push failures), making the string-matching path look blessed and inviting future copies.

### Finding 3 — But Finding 1/2 are NOT in scope for this diff; the string-match is locally consistent

`isTargetBodyLimitError` (bootstrap.go:1437) already establishes substring-matching `"http 413"` / `"body exceeded size limit"` as *this package's* accepted pattern, and `httpError` does not classify any status today. The new `isTargetPushDeadlineError` sits right beside it, mirrors its shape exactly, and `isBatchableTargetPushError` cleanly OR-composes the two. Re-architecting to typed errors would mean: a new error type in gitproto, threading the status through both `httpError` call sites (smarthttp.go:395, :499), re-exporting on the public API, and rewriting *both* the 413 and 408 detectors plus their tests — i.e. refactoring pre-existing code well beyond the timeout fix.

- Recommendation: ship as-is. The string-match is a pre-existing-pattern decision, not a regression this diff introduces; raising the altitude is a separate, larger cleanup. If anything, file a follow-up to migrate HTTP-status classification (413 + 408/504 together) into a gitproto typed error, mirroring the `RefRejectedError` work — but that does not belong in this small fix.

### Finding 4 — Conflating 408/504 (timeout) with 413 (size) under `isBatchableTargetPushError` is the right generalization, not a leaky bucket

The union is justified by what the *consumer* does with it, not by the failures being identical. Both feed exactly one decision: "subdivide into smaller packs and retry batched." The diff is careful here — it keeps `isTargetPushDeadlineError` separate from `isTargetBodyLimitError` (so size-limit *parsing*, `targetBodyLimit` at the 413 path, stays unpolluted), names the union by the *remedy* ("batchable") rather than the cause, and the comment at bootstrap.go:507-510 states the shared remedy explicitly. The only mild leak: a 408 is sometimes a transient slow-link hiccup rather than a too-big pack, so subdividing is a heuristic that can over-split a pack that wasn't actually oversized — but that degrades to "more, smaller, successful pushes" plus the recombine logic (bootstrap.go:632), which is acceptable and self-correcting.

- One-line summary: the union is keyed on shared remedy (subdivide-and-retry), with size-specific parsing kept on its own path — a sound abstraction boundary.
- Concrete cost: none structurally; worst case a transient 408 triggers unnecessary subdivision that recombine later undoes.

### Bottom line
The altitude *could* be higher (typed HTTP error in gitproto via `errors.As`, matching the brand-new `RefRejectedError`/`ErrTargetRefMoved` convention), and Finding 2 is a real architectural smell. But within this diff the new code is consistent with the package's own pre-existing `isTargetBodyLimitError` string-matching and the 408/413/504 union is a clean remedy-keyed generalization. Re-architecting would require refactoring pre-existing 413 handling and threading status through both `httpError` callers — scope creep for a timeout fix. Recommend: ship, with an optional follow-up ticket to unify HTTP-status classification into a gitproto typed error.

## What I shipped

Branch `fix/target-push-timeout-batched-retry`, three commits:

1. **`bootstrap: treat receive-pack timeouts (408/504) as batchable`** — the core fix. New `isTargetPushDeadlineError` (matches `http 408`/`http 504`) and `isBatchableTargetPushError` (body-limit OR deadline). Wired into both the one-shot auto-batch decision (`autoTargetMaxPackBytes`) and the mid-batch subdivision path, so a GitHub 408 now routes into the same batched-bootstrap fallback that 413 already used instead of hard-failing.

2. **`bootstrap: clearer messaging when a push times out`** — the auto-batch notice now distinguishes "target push timed out" from "target rejected pack", and a one-shot batchable failure that *can't* fall back (source lacks the protocol-v2 fetch filter checkpointing needs) gets wrapped with guidance instead of a bare `http 408`. Original error stays wrapped for `errors.Is`.

3. **`bootstrap: trim redundant batchable-error test cases`** — the `/simplify` cleanup.

Tests: 2 classifier table tests + `TestAutoTargetMaxPackBytesTimeoutTriggersBatching` + `TestExecuteOneShotTimeoutWithoutBatchSupportIsActionable`. Full `go build`/`go vet`/`go test ./...` green.

## `/simplify` outcome

Four parallel review agents (reuse, simplification, efficiency, altitude). Net: the diff was already clean and consistent with the package's existing `isTargetBodyLimitError` substring-matching convention.

- **Applied**: trimmed `TestIsBatchableTargetPushError` from 6 cases to 3 (the per-status edge cases are owned by the two dedicated predicate tests; the combinator test only needs to prove the OR wires both inputs).
- **Skipped, with reason**: the double `strings.ToLower(err.Error())` in the batchable check is cold-path-only (runs once per failed push) — not worth restructuring the predicates. The altitude finding (HTTP-status classification ideally lives in `gitproto` as a typed error via `errors.As`, mirroring the recent `RefRejectedError`/`ErrTargetRefMoved` work) is a legitimate architectural improvement but would require refactoring the pre-existing 413 path and threading the status code through `httpError`'s two call sites — out of scope for this fix.

One thing worth flagging for a **follow-up ticket**: unify HTTP-status classification (413 + 408/504) into a typed `gitproto` error so neither the size nor the timeout path depends on the `"http %d"` string format. Want me to open that issue, or post the workaround (`--target-max-pack-bytes`) + root cause as a comment on #70?

content.text:
Bigger — and bigger than it first looks. I traced it:

**What's genuinely easy:** `httpError` (`smarthttp.go:39`) is the *only* producer of `"http %d"` errors, and `%w`-wrapping preserves the chain all the way up through `PushPack` and the `TargetPusher` interface to bootstrap. So a typed `*HTTPStatusError{StatusCode, ...}` set in that one constructor would be reachable via `errors.As` downstream. The 408/504 check becomes a clean `err.StatusCode == 408`.

**What makes it a real refactor:**

1. **The 413 path isn't status-based, so it doesn't simplify away.** `isTargetBodyLimitError` deliberately matches *body text* — `"body exceeded size limit"`, `"request body too large"`, `"payload too large"` — because proxies return that signal under varying (often non-413) status codes, and `targetBodyLimit` parses a *number* out of that body. So the typed error would have to carry **both the status and the response body**, and the body-limit detection would still inspect the body string. That's `errors.As` *plus* string inspection — more code on that path, not less.

2. **~53 test assertions across two packages** (`bootstrap_test.go` and `syncer/integration_test.go`) construct these as plain `errors.New("body exceeded size limit …")` / `errors.New("http 413")`. Switching detection to `errors.As` breaks all of them unless I either keep a string fallback (defeats the point) or rewrite each to build the typed error. That churn — touching the syncer integration tests — is the bulk of the work.

3. It also mixes "make 408 work" with "re-architect HTTP error classification" in one PR, which muddies review and bisection, and likely means a new **public** API type (to match the `RefRejectedError`/`ErrTargetRefMoved` convention that's re-exported in `errors.go`).

**My recommendation:** keep this PR scoped to the timeout fix — it's complete and consistent with the package's own established `isTargetBodyLimitError` pattern. Do the unification as a follow-up that migrates 413 *and* 408/504 together into a typed gitproto error, with the test churn isolated to its own diff.

Want me to open that follow-up issue, or would you rather I fold it in here anyway?

gh pr create --base main --title "bootstrap: treat receive-pack timeouts (408/504) as batchable" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

Fixes #70. A one-shot bootstrap relay of a large GitLab repo to a fresh GitHub target fails with:

```
error: sync: ... target receive-pack: post RPC stream body: http 408: https://github.com/.../git-receive-pack
```

GitHub enforces a wall-clock deadline on the `git-receive-pack` POST. For a large repo (the reporter's stores compiled kernels) relayed over a slow source link, the single one-shot pack streams too slowly — the upstream read rate throttles the downstream write — and GitHub kills the request with **HTTP 408 (Request Timeout)**.

git-sync already has the right remedy for "this pack doesn't fit the target": batched bootstrap with checkpointing and auto-subdivision. But it only triggered on **413 body-limit** rejections (`isTargetBodyLimitError`). A 408 fell through as a hard, unactionable failure — even though `--target-max-pack-bytes` is the exact knob that fixes it (smaller packs each finish inside GitHub's window).

## Change

- Classify **408** (and gateway **504**) as a receive-pack *deadline* (`isTargetPushDeadlineError`) and route it through the same batched-bootstrap fallback as a size rejection, via a remedy-keyed union `isBatchableTargetPushError`. Applied to both the one-shot auto-batch decision (`autoTargetMaxPackBytes`) and the mid-batch subdivision path.
- Distinguish the cause in the auto-batch notice ("target push timed out" vs "target rejected pack").
- When a one-shot batchable failure can't fall back (source can't serve the protocol-v2 fetch filter checkpointing needs), wrap the error with guidance instead of surfacing a bare `http 408`. The original error stays wrapped for `errors.Is`.

Deadline classification is kept distinct from the body-limit path because the trigger is a timeout, not a size rejection, and there's no body limit to parse out of the message.

## Tests

- `TestIsTargetPushDeadlineError`, `TestIsBatchableTargetPushError` — classifier coverage.
- `TestAutoTargetMaxPackBytesTimeoutTriggersBatching` — a 408 now opts into batched mode.
- `TestExecuteOneShotTimeoutWithoutBatchSupportIsActionable` — actionable error when no batched fallback exists.

`go build`/`go vet`/`go test ./...` all green.

## Follow-up (not in this PR)

HTTP-status classification currently substring-matches the formatted error string, consistent with the pre-existing 413 path. A worthwhile follow-up is migrating **413 and 408/504 together** into a typed `gitproto` HTTP error inspected via `errors.As` (mirroring the recent `RefRejectedError`/`ErrTargetRefMoved` work). Kept separate here: the body-limit signal is body-text-based (not purely a status code) so it needs both status + body, and the switch would churn ~53 string-based test assertions across two packages — its own diff.
